description: Generic proof-strategy KB. Entries describe reusable goal shapes, decision criteria, and tactic categories; concrete lemma proof scripts and examples are not stored here.
how_to_use: Use strategies for roadmap-level decomposition only.
Match patterns by structural_description and indicators, then instantiate from the live EasyCrypt goal.
Use typical_tactics and reasoning_hints as generic guidance, not as a stored proof script.
Use ec_syntax and ec_tactics for tactic syntax and failure diagnosis.

strategies.description: IND chosen-plaintext security via DDH reduction: embed <G_real> inside <assumption_game>0, extract an intermediate game <G_mid> with a uniform mask, show Pr[<G_mid>]=1/2, then combine the bounds.

slot_semantics.<G_real>: the concrete IND chosen-plaintext game module
slot_semantics.<G_mid>: the intermediate game whose ciphertext uses a uniformly independent mask (induced by the <assumption_game>1 form)
slot_semantics.<reduction>: the constructed reduction module wrapping <A> to attack <assumption_game>
slot_semantics.<challenge_bit>: the uniformly sampled challenge bit variable inside <G_mid>

strategies.description: IND chosen-ciphertext security via a hardness-assumption reduction with game-hopping: embed <G_real> inside <assumption_game>0, chain intermediate games <G_mid>_i with bad events, and bound the adversary advantage. Generalizes to any chosen-ciphertext-secure scheme reduced to a hardness assumption via game hops.

slot_semantics.<G_real>: the concrete IND chosen-ciphertext game module
slot_semantics.<G_mid>: family of intermediate games (G1, G2, ...) used as way-stations in the hop chain
slot_semantics.<assumption_game>: the hardness-assumption experiment family with variants 0/1 (e.g. DDH0_ex / DDH1_ex)
slot_semantics.<A_neg>: the adversary variant that returns the negation of A's guess (used to remove the 1/2)
slot_semantics.<reduction>: the reduction module wrapping <A> to attack <assumption_game>
slot_semantics.<shared_state>: the set of ghost variables shared by both sides inside the oracle (e.g. the decryption log, the target ciphertext, and the secret key)
slot_semantics.<oracle_equiv_lemma>: a pre-proved local equiv lemma that the 2-arg call uses to discharge oracle subgoals
slot_semantics.<bad_var>: the boolean bad-event variable tracked by the instrumented game
slot_semantics.<Inv>: the full-state invariant preserved by both oracles outside the bad branch
slot_semantics.<bound>: the concrete probability bound on the bad event (e.g. q_dec/|order|)
slot_semantics.<reparam_pair>: a pair of samplings that must be coupled via an interleaved swap -1; rnd (bij); rnd; wp sequence because one variable is an affine function of the other
slot_semantics.<tcr_bound>: the advantage bound of the auxiliary assumption used in the final theorem (e.g. target-collision-resistance)
slot_semantics.<starting_lemma>: auxiliary inequality lemma instantiated with `&m` that the final have-chain starts from (e.g., an `aux1 &m` bounding Pr[<G_real>] by an intermediate-game Pr-term plus a constant)
slot_semantics.<prog_0>: the full Pr-bracket program term `Module.method() @ &m` on the LHS of the first hop's rewrite
slot_semantics.<prog_1>: the full Pr-bracket program term that is both the RHS of the first hop and the LHS of the second
slot_semantics.<prog_2>: the full Pr-bracket program term on the RHS of the second hop
slot_semantics.<event>: the common event expression carried across hops (typically `res` or `res \/ <bad_var>`)
slot_semantics.<hop_lemma_1>: the byequiv lemma name discharging the first game-hop rewrite
slot_semantics.<hop_lemma_2>: the byequiv lemma name discharging the second game-hop rewrite

variants.name: have_chain_final_assembly
variants.conditions: A family of per-hop byequiv lemmas has been pre-proved (each discharging one game-hop's `Pr[<prog_i> : <event>] = Pr[<prog_{i+1}> : <event>]`), together with an auxiliary inequality lemma `<starting_lemma>` providing the initial bound. The top-level theorem is assembled as a flat `have`-chain that instantiates `<starting_lemma>` and successively rewrites the intermediate Pr-term through the pre-proved hops (`+ by byequiv <hop_lemma_i>`), accumulating side-bounds, finishing with `smt (mu_bounded)`. Applies when the variant-reduction proof is structured as many small lemmas rather than one long byequiv.

phases.goal: Negated-adversary bridge: Pr[<G_real>(A) @ &m : res] = 1 - Pr[<G_real>(<A_neg>) @ &m : res]
Negated-adversary bridge: Pr[<G_real>(A) @ &m : res] = 1 - Pr[<G_real>(<A_neg>) @ &m : res]

strategies.description: Prove <op_dec>(<op_enc>(m)) = m for an encryption or commitment scheme

slot_semantics.<correctness_game>: the correctness game functor applied to the scheme
slot_semantics.<op_enc>: the encrypt procedure on the scheme
slot_semantics.<op_dec>: the decrypt procedure on the scheme

strategies.description: Perfect hiding for a commitment scheme: commitment reveals no information about the message

slot_semantics.<hiding_lemma>: the top-level perfect-hiding lemma that composes the two intermediate bounds
slot_semantics.<pr_eq_lemma>: the intermediate lemma showing Pr[Real(A)] = Pr[Ideal(A)]

phases.goal: Pr[Real(A)] = Pr[Ideal(A)] -- real and ideal games are equivalent
Pr[Ideal(A)] = 1/2 -- ideal game is a coin flip

strategies.description: Computational binding: extracting a discrete log witness from a binding break

phases.goal: algebraic extraction: from two openings (m,d) and (m',d') with m!=m', extract x = (d-d')/(m'-m)

strategies.description: Sigma protocol verification: completeness, special soundness, and SHVZK

phases.goal: completeness: R h w => Pr[Completeness : res] = 1
special_soundness: extract witness from two transcripts

strategies.description: Game-hopping security proof: chain of intermediate games with bounds at each hop

phases.goal: For each hop: Pr[G_i] = Pr[G_{i+1}] or |Pr[G_i] - Pr[G_{i+1}]| <= bound_i
Combine all hops: have h0 := hop0 &m; have h1 := hop1 &m; ...; smt()

strategies.description: Prove that a program output is uniformly distributed over a set

phases.goal: Pr[M : output = x] = f(x) for all x -- compute the exact distribution
equiv: output distributions are identical for different inputs

strategies.description: <scheme>-correctness via lossless + hoare while loop with XOR invariant

slot_semantics.<loop_inv>: the loop invariant predicate (typically an XOR identity over big sums)
slot_semantics.<loop_bounds>: side conjuncts on the loop counter(s), e.g. 0 <= j <= N
slot_semantics.<loop_variant>: the variant expression that strictly decreases (e.g. N - j)

strategies.description: Prove Pr[<G_real>(<A_neg>) @ &m : res] = 1 - Pr[<G_real>(A) @ &m : res] where <A_neg> negates A's guess output. Split into byequiv + rewrite Pr[mu_not] + losslessness.

slot_semantics.<G_real>: the security experiment game module parameterized by the adversary
slot_semantics.<A_neg>: the adversary wrapper that runs A and returns the negation of A's guess
slot_semantics.<oracle_state>: the set of ghost variables representing the oracle's shared state (identical on both sides)
slot_semantics.<A_ll>: the losslessness axiom/lemma for the abstract adversary procedure

ec_syntax.description: Rewrite Pr[M @ &m : !E] to Pr[M @ &m : true] - Pr[M @ &m : E]
ec_syntax.syntax: rewrite Pr [mu_not]
ec_syntax.requires: Losslessness proof to simplify Pr[M : true] = 1

ec_syntax.description: Rewrite Pr[M @ &m : E = true] to Pr[M @ &m : E] (remove trivial = true)
ec_syntax.syntax: Various approaches: /= or rewrite eqT or congr
ec_syntax.requires: Goal has (E = true) or (E = false) inside Pr

ec_syntax.description: Reorder program statements in pRHL goals
ec_syntax.syntax: swap{side} pos offset -- moves statement at <pos> forward by <offset> positions on <side> (1=left, 2=right). Also: swap{side} [from..to] offset for ranges.
ec_syntax.requires: Statements must be independent (no read/write dependency). Read the goal state to count statement positions.
ec_syntax.error_fix: Check which variable is read/written. Try swapping on the other side ({2} instead of {1}), or use seq to split the program at a safe point.

ec_syntax.description: Discard an extra sampling on one side of a byequiv
ec_syntax.syntax: rnd{1} or rnd{2}
ec_syntax.requires: One side has a sampling that the other doesn't

ec_syntax.description: Split a phoare goal into losslessness + hoare, then combine
ec_syntax.syntax: have Hll : islossless M.proc. ... have Hh : hoare [...]. ... by conseq Hll Hh.
ec_syntax.requires: A phoare goal that is easier to prove as separate lossless + functional correctness. CRITICAL: Hll must be an `islossless M.proc` pHL judgment, NOT a `Pr[M.proc @ &m : true] = 1%r` probability bound. These look similar but are different types. Using a Pr bound as Hll causes: 'do not know how to combine hoareF and _ and _ into phoareF'.

ec_syntax.description: Force a conditional branch in a program to take the true or false path
ec_syntax.syntax: rcondt{side} pos -- force the if-then-else at statement <pos> to take the true branch (generates a sub-goal to prove the condition is true). rcondf for the false branch.
ec_syntax.requires: A pRHL goal with an if-then-else. Read the goal state to find which statement position the conditional is at.

ec_syntax.description: Split a pRHL goal at specific program positions
ec_syntax.syntax: seq left_count right_count : (intermediate_condition). -- splits the pRHL proof at a program point, proving the first <left_count> statements on the left match the first <right_count> on the right.
ec_syntax.requires: When call or sim can't handle the full program -- split into two parts. Read the goal state to count statements on each side.

ec_syntax.description: Cancel multiplication to perform field division in exponent algebra
ec_syntax.syntax: GP.ZModE.ZModpField.mulrK or ZPF.mulrK
ec_syntax.requires: A goal of the form x * y / y = x or equivalent in a ZModField

ec_syntax.description: One-shot tactic to prove islossless goals. Handles proc + inline + rnd predT chain automatically.
ec_syntax.syntax: islossless
ec_syntax.requires: Goal must be an islossless judgment. All distributions must be lossless.

ec_syntax.description: Close group/ring/field equalities without smt hints
ec_syntax.syntax: algebra or ring or field
ec_syntax.requires: Goal is a polynomial equality in a ring or field (group exponents, ZModField, etc.)

ec_syntax.description: Close trivial arithmetic/logic goals with /# (crush)
ec_syntax.requires: Goal is a trivial arithmetic or logic obligation (e.g., after byequiv => //)

ec_syntax.description: Bound probability of membership: Pr[x in L] <= |L| * max_mu1
ec_syntax.syntax: apply (ler_trans ((size l)%r * r)). + by apply mu_mem_le_mu1. apply ler_wpmul2r; smt(mu_bounded). by apply le_fromint.
ec_syntax.requires: Goal is a probability bound involving set membership and distribution weight

ec_syntax.description: Reference current precondition in seq/while conditions with #pre
ec_syntax.syntax: #pre
ec_syntax.requires: Inside a seq or while condition where you want to extend the current precondition

ec_syntax.description: When a lemma is exported from a section with a module declaration (e.g., `declare module A : Adv`), applying it outside the section may fail with 'cannot infer module arguments'.
ec_syntax.syntax: apply (lemma_name Module). -- pass the module explicitly
ec_syntax.requires: Error: 'cannot infer module arguments' when applying a section-exported lemma

ec_syntax.description: After `clone Theory as Alias`, lemma names from Theory are renamed under the Alias prefix. The original names are no longer in scope. CRITICAL: when unqualified names are used in `rewrite !name`, EC silently makes zero rewrites (no error, no progress) rather than reporting an error.
ec_syntax.syntax: Alias.lemma_name
ec_syntax.requires: Error: 'unknown identifier' when using a lemma from a cloned theory; OR `rewrite !name` succeeds without changing the goal (silent zero-rewrite).

ec_syntax.description: Close group/field exponent equalities in one shot. Handles expressions like g^a^b = g^(a*b), g^a * g^b = g^(a+b), and complex multi-variable group algebra.
ec_syntax.syntax: algebra
ec_syntax.requires: Goal is a group/ring/field equality over exponents (ZModField). Works on goals left by `auto => />` in equiv proofs.

ec_syntax.description: Normalize group exponent expressions using logarithm bijection and ring arithmetic. Converts nested exponentiation (g^a^b, (g^a * g^b)^c) to flat form (g^(a*b), g^(a*c + b*c)).
ec_syntax.syntax: have -> : <nested_group_expr> = <flat_group_expr> by rewrite log_bij !(logg1, logrzM, logDr); ring.
ec_syntax.requires: Goal contains group expressions with nested exponentiation that need to be shown equal. Common after `auto => />` in byequiv proofs.

ec_syntax.description: Close goals of the form (a = b) = (b = a) using eq_iff.
ec_syntax.syntax: rewrite eq_iff
ec_syntax.requires: Goal has a boolean equality with swapped sides, typically after `do !split` in an equiv postcondition.

ec_syntax.description: Prove oracle equivalence in byequiv proofs. THREE forms depending on context: (A) identical oracle with shared state, (B) oracle with bad-event tracking and a local equiv lemma, (C) oracle with bad-event tracking proved inline.
ec_syntax.syntax:
(A) call (_: ={state}). + by sim.
(B) call (_: bad_var, Inv). + by apply local_lemma.
(C) call (_: bad_var, Inv). + by proc; inline *; auto => />; ...
ec_syntax.requires: A byequiv goal with adversary/oracle calls on both sides.

ec_syntax.description: The `call` tactic with bad-event coupling has a 2-argument and 3-argument form. They generate DIFFERENT oracle subgoals with different module path handling. Using the wrong form causes `apply` to fail.
ec_syntax.syntax:
2-arg: call (_: bad_var, Inv).
3-arg: call (_: bad_var, Inv, frame).
ec_syntax.requires: A byequiv goal with adversary/oracle call and bad-event tracking.

ec_syntax.description: Coupling for reparameterized samplings in game hops. When one game samples (x1, x2) directly and the other computes x1 = x - w*x2, use interleaved swap/rnd/wp per variable pair instead of auto.
ec_syntax.syntax: swap -1. rnd (fun z => z + G1.w{2} * G1.x2{2}) (fun z => z - G1.w{2} * G1.x2{2}). rnd. wp.
ec_syntax.requires: A pRHL goal where one side has reparameterized samplings (e.g., G1 computes G1.x1 = G1.x - w*G1.x2 after sampling G1.x and G1.x2 independently).

ec_syntax.description: In pRHL `rnd (f) (g)` bijection functions, any variable reference is safe as long as the referenced variable is still in the program (not yet consumed by rnd) when the rnd executes. Both {1} locals and {2} module globals work. The danger is referencing a variable that has ALREADY been consumed by a previous rnd — it becomes a free variable separate from the quantified coupling variable.
ec_syntax.syntax:
SAFE: rnd (fun z => z + G1.w{2} * G1.z2{2}) ...  (* G1.z2 still in program *)
SAFE: rnd (fun z => z + G1.w{2} * z2{1}) ...    (* z2 still in program *)
SAFE: rnd (fun z => z / x{1}) ...                (* x still in program *)
UNSAFE: rnd coupled z2 first, THEN rnd (fun z => z + w * z2{1}) ... (* z2{1} already consumed *)
ec_syntax.requires: A pRHL rnd with bijection where the bijection function references other program variables.
ec_syntax.error_fix: Use `swap -1` before the bijection rnd to ensure the referenced variable is still alive. See swap_minus_one_idiom.

ec_syntax.description: The `swap -1` idiom for reparameterized sampling coupling. Before each bijection rnd, use `swap -1` to swap the last two left-side samplings so the bijection variable (z1) is below the identity variable (z2). This is simpler than computing absolute swap positions.
ec_syntax.syntax: swap -1; rnd (bijection); rnd; wp.
ec_syntax.requires: A pRHL goal where the last two left-side samplings are a variable pair (e.g., z1 then z2) that need coupled with a reparameterized pair on the right (G1.z then G1.z2).

ec_syntax.description: The `split` tactic behaves differently on Prop-level `/\` vs boolean `&&`. On `&&`, `split` alone silently does nothing (no error, no effect). Use `split => [intro1 | intro2]` with intro patterns to decompose `&&`.
ec_syntax.syntax: split => [? _ | eqName]; 1: by field.
ec_syntax.requires: Goal contains a boolean `&&` conjunction, typically after `auto =>` in pRHL postcondition closure.

ec_syntax.description: The `field` tactic needs an explicit `x <> zero` hypothesis. After `supp_dexcepted`, the non-zero condition is in the form `! pred1 zero x` which `field` cannot use. Use `rewrite /pred1` to unfold it.
ec_syntax.syntax: rewrite /pred1 => -[] InxL HxL.
ec_syntax.requires: A hypothesis like `(xL \in dt) /\ ! pred1 zero xL` in context, typically produced by `move: H => /supp_dexcepted.` after a `dt \ pred1 zero` sampling.

ec_syntax.description: Convert boolean negation `!P args` into propositional form `P args -> false` using `apply/negP`, enabling introduction and case analysis on an inductively-defined predicate P. The pattern `apply/negP. move=> h. case: h.` is the canonical idiom for destructuring a negated inductive predicate.
ec_syntax.syntax: apply/negP. move=> h_bad. case: h_bad.
ec_syntax.requires: Goal is `! P args` where P is an inductively-defined predicate (declared with `inductive`). Cannot directly introduce or case-split on `! P` without converting to propositional form first.

ec_syntax.description: Use `case: h.` (not `elim: h.`) to perform case analysis on a hypothesis `h: P args` where P is an inductively-defined predicate. `elim: h.` fails with '[error] don't know what to eliminate' because `elim:` is for structural induction on inductive TYPES (nat, list), not predicates defined with `inductive`.
ec_syntax.syntax:
case: h_bad.    (* correct: opens one subgoal per constructor *)
elim: h_bad.    (* WRONG: '[error] don't know what to eliminate' *)
ec_syntax.requires: Hypothesis `h: P args` where P is defined with the `inductive` keyword (e.g., `inductive Bad logP m = | Cycle of ... | Collision of ...`). After `apply/negP. move=> h_bad.`, use `case: h_bad.` to split on constructors.

ec_syntax.description: When case-splitting on a boolean expression with `case: (bool_expr)`, no new named hypothesis is produced — the boolean value is substituted into the goal. Adding an intro pattern like `h` after the `=>` causes '[error] invalid intro-pattern: nothing to introduce'. Use the pattern without intro, or use `apply/negP` to convert a boolean negation into a propositional form where introduction is valid.
ec_syntax.syntax:
case: (bool_expr) => //=.            (* correct: no intro, just closes trivial cases *)
case: (bool_expr) => //= h.          (* WRONG: 'nothing to introduce' *)
(* Alternative for the non-trivial branch: introduce a proof via move=> first, then apply/negP *)
move=> h_mem; apply/negP => h_neg.   (* correct: h_mem from first intro, h_neg from negP flip *)
ec_syntax.requires: Goal contains a boolean subexpression to case-split on. The split substitutes the boolean value into the goal rather than creating a named hypothesis. This differs from splitting on a Prop, which does introduce named hypotheses.

ec_syntax.description: Lemma names containing apostrophes (e.g., Exp', G'') cause shell quoting corruption in session_cli -next/-chain -c '...' commands. The apostrophe in the session directory name (-d argument) prematurely terminates the single-quoted -c argument, silently appending shell tokens (like 2>&1) into the tactic string, causing parse errors.
ec_syntax.syntax:
# WRONG: directory contains apostrophe
python3 session_cli.py -d .ec_session_Exp'_1 -next -c 'byequiv=> //.'
# Shell sees: -c 'byequiv=> //.' 2>&1' (apostrophe in dir name closes the quote!)
# Tactic received by EasyCrypt: byequiv=> //. 2>&1 -> parse error

# CORRECT option 1: sanitize directory name (replace apostrophe with _prime)
python3 session_cli.py -d .ec_session_Exp_prime_1 -next -c 'byequiv=> //.'

# CORRECT option 2: use double-quotes for the -c value
python3 session_cli.py -d .ec_session_<game>_prime -next -c "byequiv=> //."
ec_syntax.requires: Lemma name or any CLI argument contains single-quote characters (apostrophes). Common EasyCrypt names: Exp', G', G1', and any prime-variant of a module or game.
ec_syntax.error_fix: Check the session directory name (-d argument) for apostrophes or other shell-special characters. Rename to a sanitized form without special characters. Re-issue the failing tactic command using the sanitized name.

ec_syntax.description: When `byequiv=> //.` is accepted by EasyCrypt but the resulting sub-goal cannot be closed, the default result-equality postcondition is too weak. Supply an explicit postcondition naming the shared internal state variables the proof needs to track. Required when the event in the outer Pr[...] goal is defined over module state rather than the return value, and the two games share that state through structurally different procedures.
ec_syntax.syntax: byequiv (_: ={glob A} ==> ={Mod1.var1, Mod2.var2})=> //.
ec_syntax.requires: The outer lemma proves Pr[G1 @ &m : event] = Pr[G2 @ &m : event] where event is a predicate over internal module state (not just res). The two games must share those state variables through their oracle calls, even if their top-level procedure structures differ.
ec_syntax.error_fix: Identify which module state variables appear in the event expression (e.g., `Bad P.logP F.m` touches P.logP and F.m). Supply them in the postcondition: `byequiv (_: ={glob A} ==> ={P.logP, F.m})=> //.` The precondition `={glob A}` (adversary state equal at start) is standard for security games.

ec_syntax.description: The while tactic for phoare goals takes TWO arguments (invariant, variant); EasyCrypt infers probability=1%r automatically. The three-argument form `while (inv) (var) 1%r` causes a parse error at the `1%r` token in some EC versions. The hoare while rule takes ONE argument (invariant only — no variant). Using two args on a hoare goal causes an `invalid arguments` arity error.
ec_syntax.syntax:
while (invariant) (variant_expr)   -- phoare (2 args, EC infers prob=1%r)
while (invariant)                    -- hoare  (1 arg, no variant needed)
ec_syntax.requires: Goal is a phoare or hoare judgment with a while loop.

ec_syntax.description: When a goal contains `big predT F (x::s)` over a cloned or abstract type (e.g., word), smt fails even when big_consT is passed as a hint. The rewrite must happen explicitly before smt.
ec_syntax.syntax: rewrite !big_consT /=
ec_syntax.requires: Goal contains big-sum terms over cons-lists (e.g., `big predT F (elem::tail)`). Type is a cloned algebraic structure (word, etc.).

ec_syntax.description: Close the field denominator non-zero side condition arising in discrete log extraction proofs. CRITICAL: rewrite log-form hypotheses into the goal BEFORE calling field, so the denominator normalizes to x - y (not (-y) + x), enabling the subr_eq0 rewrite to close the side condition.
ec_syntax.syntax: rewrite Hr_l Hr'_l. field. rewrite GP.ZModE.ZModpRing.subr_eq0; exact Hneq.
ec_syntax.requires: Goal involves field division where the denominator is ch - ch' from scalar subtraction; hypothesis Hneq: ch <> ch' is in context; log-form hypotheses expressing responses as linear functions of loge h (Hr_l, Hr'_l) have been proved with have.

ec_syntax.description: Rewrite with a lemma that requires a proof argument alongside term arguments. EasyCrypt's rewrite tactic does not accept proof hypotheses as trailing explicit arguments when wildcards occupy the term positions — the total argument count exceeds what rewrite expects. The fix: materialize the instantiated equation via have, then rewrite with the local hypothesis.
ec_syntax.syntax: have H := lemma arg1 arg2 ... proof_hyp; rewrite H
ec_syntax.requires: Lemma has type `A -> B -> ... -> (membership_or_typing_proof) -> (lhs = rhs)`. Attempting `rewrite (lemma _ _ ... proof_hyp)` fails with 'too many arguments'.

ec_syntax.description: Prove mu1 [a..b] x = mu1 [a..b] y when both x and y are known to be in [a..b]. Use dinter1E (unconditional equation form) NOT supp_dinter1E (conditional implication) or dinter_uni (uniformity predicate). dinter1E unfolds both sides to the same if-expression, which closes by reflexivity once the bound hypotheses are substituted.
ec_syntax.syntax: rewrite !dinter1E Hx Hy.
ec_syntax.requires: Hypotheses Hx: a <= x <= b and Hy: a <= y <= b in context. Goal shape: mu1 [a..b] x = mu1 [a..b] y.

ec_syntax.description: Use an iff hypothesis hf: A <=> B to derive A from a hypothesis HB: B (backward direction). Correct: `move: HB; rewrite -hf` or `apply hf`. Wrong: `rewrite hf` (forward direction) which transforms the goal A into B, then the `by` block has no closer.
ec_syntax.syntax:
have Ha: A by (move: HB; rewrite -hf).
(* or *)
have Ha: A by apply hf.
ec_syntax.requires: Hypothesis hf: A <=> B and hypothesis HB: B in context. Goal (or sub-goal of a have) requires proving A.

ec_syntax.description: Rewrite a Pr[M @ &m : E] term by substituting a closed-form equation via `have -> :=`. The standard `rewrite (prLemma args)` tactic is accepted by EasyCrypt but produces NO_PROGRESS on Pr[...] terms because probability expressions are opaque to the rewrite engine. The fix: introduce the equation as a local rewrite hypothesis with `have -> := prLemma args`, which rewrites the Pr term in place.
ec_syntax.syntax: have -> := prLemma arg1 arg2 ... argN.
ec_syntax.requires: A lemma `prLemma : forall args, Pr[M @ &m : E] = expr` (a probability equality proved e.g. via byphoare). The lemma must match the Pr[...] term in the goal.

ec_syntax.description: Prove Pr[M @ &m : P res] = Pr[M @ &m : Q res] when P and Q are equivalent post-conditions related by a bijection (e.g., `finv res = a` vs `res = f a`). Strategy: introduce as a `have ->` goal, prove via byequiv coupling M with itself (identity coupling on shared loop state), then close the bijection ambient goal with smt().
ec_syntax.syntax:
have ->: Pr[M @ &m : P res] = Pr[M @ &m : Q res].
byequiv (_: ={arg} ==> (P res{1}) = (Q res{2})) => //.
proc; while (={shared_loop_vars}).
auto.   (* while body *)
auto.   (* while init *)
smt().  (* ambient bijection goal *)
move=> _ _ _; smt().
ec_syntax.requires: Both sides are the SAME program M. P and Q are related by a bijection so that `P r = Q r` is provable by smt() with bijection hypotheses (hf, hfinv) in scope. The loop invariant is equality of all shared loop variables.

ec_syntax.description: After `while (Inv)` in a pRHL (byequiv) proof, use plain `auto.` for the while body subgoal and the init subgoal. Do NOT use `auto => />` — the view simplification can eliminate patterns needed by subsequent tactics and produces ambient goals harder to close. Follow with `smt()` for the ambient bijection or logic goal. For post-loop goals requiring explicit intro, use `move=>` then `smt()`.
ec_syntax.syntax:
proc; while (={shared_vars} /\ invariant).
auto.    (* body subgoal *)
auto.    (* init subgoal *)
smt().   (* ambient bijection/logic goal *)
ec_syntax.requires: A pRHL goal `M ~ M : pre ==> post` (same program, identity coupling). The loop body is structurally identical on both sides. The invariant tracks shared loop variables. Outer-scope bijection hypotheses (e.g., hf, hfinv) are accessible to smt() without naming them as hints.

ec_syntax.description: When a lemma has an outer variable (e.g., i0) and the procedure uses a different argument name (e.g., i), plain `byphoare => //` leaves them as separate free variables. Use `byphoare (_: i = i0 /\ additional_pre ==> _) => //` to explicitly bind them. This allows `-> ` substitution in subsequent intro patterns and ensures the while invariant can use i0 throughout.
ec_syntax.syntax: byphoare (_: proc_arg = outer_var /\ additional_precond ==> _) => //.
ec_syntax.requires: Goal is `Pr[M.proc(proc_arg) @ &m : post] = bound`. The outer lemma has a universally quantified variable `outer_var` that is not syntactically the same as `proc_arg`.

ec_syntax.description: EasyCrypt does not support Coq-style `rewrite X in H.`. To rewrite inside a hypothesis H using equation X, push H onto the goal stack, apply the rewrite, then re-introduce it.
ec_syntax.syntax: move: H; rewrite X /= => H.
ec_syntax.requires: Hypothesis H in context contains a subterm that equation X can rewrite. Goal need not mention H directly.

ec_syntax.description: When session_cli is started with `-f <target_file>`, only lemmas and axioms defined BEFORE the target lemma in the file are in scope. Definitions appearing after the target lemma (even in the same file) are NOT available, even though they appear in the same file.
ec_syntax.syntax: (awareness only — no tactic fix; check file positions before using a lemma as an smt hint)
ec_syntax.requires: Using session_cli -f <target_file> to prove a lemma that may reference axioms or lemmas declared later in the file.

ec_syntax.description: When applying `byequiv H` where H is a local equiv hypothesis (created via `have H := EquivLemma args`), EasyCrypt silently accepts the tactic but makes NO PROGRESS if the goal's Pr[...] program matches the RIGHT side of H's equiv. `byequiv` expects the goal's program to be on the LEFT of the equiv — if it is on the right, the tactic is a silent no-op, not an error. Similarly, `rewrite -(prDX k &m)` on a goal about the wrong program also makes no progress.
ec_syntax.syntax:
# WRONG (silent no-op when goal is about the right-side program):
have H := EquivLemma args.   (* H: equiv [left_prog ~ right_prog : ...] *)
byequiv H => //.              (* accepted, but goal unchanged *)

# CORRECT — use have H + rewrite -H (see byequiv_direction_have_naming):
have H : Pr[left_prog @ &m : E] = Pr[right_prog @ &m : E].
  byequiv (EquivLemma args) => //.
rewrite -H.
apply prLeft.
ec_syntax.requires: A local equiv hypothesis or equiv lemma where the goal's program is the RIGHT side. Goal is `Pr[right_prog @ &m : E] = <closed_form>` but the equiv has left_prog on the left.

ec_syntax.description: Use `inline *` (not named `inline Mod.proc`) to inline concrete procedure calls in pRHL goals when any called procedure comes from a functor-instantiated module. Functor-applied procedures (e.g., CPA_game(Adv, Oracle).main) are not addressable by their qualified name; EasyCrypt reports 'unknown procedure: Mod.proc' for any named inline attempt on them.
ec_syntax.syntax: inline *.
ec_syntax.requires: pRHL goal where at least one program calls a procedure from a module instantiated by functor application (e.g., CPA_game(Adv, Oracle) or CCA_CPA_Adv(BNR_Adv(A))).
ec_syntax.error_fix: Replace the named inline with `inline *`. Do not spend time searching the file for the procedure name — functor-instantiated module procedures are intentionally not in scope under those qualified names. The procedure exists and is callable, but its EasyCrypt-internal name is resolved through the functor application, not the qualified path.

ec_syntax.description: When using byequiv to prove a probability equality, the direction of the resulting equality is determined by the equiv lemma's program order: if the lemma is `equiv [X ~ Y : ...]`, byequiv proves `Pr[X @ &m : E] = Pr[Y @ &m : E]`. If your goal has Pr[Y...] on the left (or needs the reverse direction), use a named hypothesis `have H: Pr[X @ &m : E] = Pr[Y @ &m : E]` (matching the equiv direction), then `rewrite -H` to substitute in the correct direction in the main goal. Avoid `have -> : Pr[Y...] = Pr[X...]` — the byequiv subgoal will be in the wrong direction.
ec_syntax.syntax:
have H: Pr[left_program @ &m : E] = Pr[right_program @ &m : E].
  byequiv (equiv_lemma ...) => //.
rewrite -H.
ec_syntax.requires: An equiv lemma `equiv [left_program ~ right_program : ...]` in scope.

patterns.structural_description: Single-program probability-one goal (Pr[G]=1) where the procedure contains a while loop with a non-trivial correctness invariant. Direct byphoare gets stuck because losslessness and correctness are entangled in the phoare judgment.
indicators: Pr[M.main(...) @ &m : post] = 1%r
procedure body has while loop
post-condition involves state accumulated across loop iterations
reasoning_hints: The key insight is SEPARATION: split Pr[...]=1 into (1) losslessness (easy) and (2) hoare correctness (hard) via conseq, then handle each independently.
For while loops with threshold-dependent behavior (e.g., loop behaves differently before and after index i), use a TWO-PHASE case-split invariant: `if j <= i then Phase1 else Phase2`.
Use `conseq (_: _ ==> semantic_pred) => //` to bridge from the raw post-condition to a cleaner semantic predicate, then prove the bridge separately.
The losslessness half is always the same pattern: while (true) (N-j) + rnd predT + smt(dist_ll).
patterns.common_failure: Trying to find one while-loop invariant that simultaneously proves losslessness AND correctness. This is much harder than proving them separately.
patterns.fallback: If the while loop is simple enough, direct byphoare without conseq may work: byphoare => //; proc; inline *; auto => />; smt(...).

patterns.structural_description: While loop where behavior changes at a threshold index (e.g., processing element i in a list of N elements). Before the threshold, one property holds; after it, a different property holds.
indicators: while loop with counter j from 0 to N
conditional inside loop body: if (j = i) or if (j < i)
state accumulation differs before/after the threshold
reasoning_hints: The invariant must handle THREE cases in the loop body: (1) j < threshold (before), (2) j = threshold (transition), (3) j > threshold (after).
At the transition point (j = threshold), the 'before' invariant provides the precondition needed to establish the 'after' invariant.
Use `case: (j{m} = threshold) => [->|Hneq]` to split the threshold case, then `subst` or `rewrite` to simplify.
Common pattern: before threshold, lists are equal; after threshold, lists satisfy an XOR/sum/difference relation.
patterns.common_failure: Using a single invariant without case split. The invariant becomes too complex and smt/auto cannot close the loop body obligation.
patterns.fallback: If the loop is simple (no threshold behavior), a single invariant suffices: while (simple_inv) (N-j).

patterns.structural_description: Two-sided equiv (Pr[G1]=Pr[G2]) where after proc;inline*, programs have aligned structure but sampling order differs, or one side has extra masking (OTP). Needs swap to align, then rnd bijection.
indicators: Two-column program after inline*
Both sides have <$ sampling
One side has extra multiplication/XOR on a sampled value
typical_tactics: rnd (fun x => x + mask) (fun x => x - mask)
patterns.common_failure: swap_not_independent
patterns.fallback: Check read/write dependencies between statements. Try swapping on the other side (swap{2}) if swap{1} fails. If statements truly depend, use seq to split the program at a safe point (read goal to determine statement counts). For complex closings after auto, write conseq with explicit postcondition BEFORE auto, not after.
reasoning_hints: Compare left/right programs side by side to find sampling misalignment
swap{side} pos offset moves the statement at <pos> forward by <offset> positions on the given side (1=left, 2=right)
You must read the current goal state to determine the correct N and M -- count statement positions after inline *
wp consumes deterministic assignments from the bottom up

patterns.structural_description: Two-sided equiv where programs are structurally identical or nearly so. sim or call+sim closes it.
indicators: Two-column program after inline*
Left and right programs have same structure
No sampling order mismatch

patterns.structural_description: Two-sided equiv where programs have a while loop and coupling is done inside the loop body via rnd bijection. Two sub-cases: (A) precondition equates the differing parameter across sides — identity or simple bijection suffices, loop invariant includes the parameter; (B) precondition is 'true' (differing parameters allowed) — the sampling result's effect on output depends on whichever value the parameter has on each side, so a conditional bijection must compensate for the parameter difference, and the loop invariant must NOT include the differing parameter.
indicators: while loop in program
rnd with conditional bijection inside loop body
precondition is 'true' or does not fix a parameter that governs a conditional inside the loop

slot_semantics.<loop_inv>: the loop invariant (omit the differing parameter when precondition is true)
slot_semantics.<cond_l>: the boolean condition on side {1} that governs the conditional branch inside the loop body
slot_semantics.<cond_r>: the boolean condition on side {2} that governs the conditional branch inside the loop body

reasoning_hints: Sub-case A (same parameter): loop invariant includes the parameter (={shared_vars, param}); identity coupling or simple bijection works because both programs behave symmetrically.

Sub-case B (differing parameter, true precondition): do NOT put the differing parameter in the loop invariant. The coupling function must compensate: if the parameter value causes the same conditional branch on both sides, pass b through; otherwise flip b. Pattern: rnd (fun b => if (condition_left) = (condition_right) then b else !b).
Choosing identity coupling (fun b => b) when the precondition is 'true' will fail: the bijection does not equate the distributions when the parameter differs across sides.

The 'condition_left = condition_right' equality check determines whether the loop variable is on the same side of the differing parameter on both executions — if yes, no flip needed; if no, flip compensates.
patterns.common_failure: identity_coupling_insufficient
patterns.fallback: If rnd (fun b => b) or simple bijection fails, check whether the precondition is 'true' (inputs can differ). If so, identify the conditional inside the loop that depends on the differing parameter, and write a conditional bijection: rnd (fun b => if (cond{1}) = (cond{2}) then b else !b). Drop the differing parameter from the loop invariant.

patterns.structural_description: Prove Pr[game]=1/2 via byphoare. The game returns b'=b where b is uniform and independent of adversary. Use swap to move b to end, rnd (pred1 b'), then show all prior steps are lossless.
indicators: Pr[...] = 1%r/2%r in goal
Program has b <$ {0,1} and b' <@ A.guess(...)
patterns.common_failure: cannot_prove_goal_strict
patterns.fallback: Ensure all adversary calls are consumed (via call + losslessness lemma) before rnd (pred1 b'). The rnd must be the very last sampling.
reasoning_hints: The key insight: b is sampled AFTER the adversary runs, so b is independent of b'
Use swap to move the uniform bit sampling to the very end of the program so rnd (pred1 ...) can consume it
You must read the goal state to find which position the bit sampling is at and compute the swap offset
rnd (pred1 guess_var) says: for any fixed guess, Pr[bit = guess] = 1/2 because bit is uniform
All prior procedure calls must be shown lossless (call oracle_ll for each oracle) before rnd consumes the bit

patterns.structural_description: Prove a probability or hoare property about a program with a while loop. Need loop invariant + variant for termination.
indicators: Program has while loop
byphoare or hoare goal
Need to track loop counter and accumulated state

slot_semantics.<invariant>: the loop invariant predicate (a property preserved by the loop body)
slot_semantics.<variant>: the loop variant (a natural-number expression that strictly decreases each iteration)
slot_semantics.<bounds>: side conjuncts on the loop counter(s), e.g. 0 <= j <= N
slot_semantics.<smt_bag>: the bag of smt hints used to close the residual algebraic subgoal

patterns.common_failure: cannot_prove_goal_strict
patterns.fallback: Strengthen the loop invariant. Common missing pieces: loop counter bounds (0 <= j <= N), index constraints (0 <= i0 < N).
reasoning_hints: The invariant must hold at loop entry (auto closes the base case)
The variant must decrease and be non-negative
For XOR-based invariants: big predT a s +^ big predT a s' = target
sp (simplify program) before while moves assignments into context

HOARE while takes ONE arg (<invariant> only). PHOARE while takes TWO args (<invariant>, <variant>). The 3-arg phoare form `while (<invariant>) (<variant>) 1%r.` causes a syntax error in some EC versions — use 2 args, probability is inferred. Using 2 args on a HOARE goal causes 'invalid arguments' — use 1 arg.

After `while (<invariant>); auto.` on a hoare goal, the loop body obligation has shape `((<invariant> /\ rest) /\ guard) ==> ...` — introduce with `[[Hinv Hrest] HjN2]` (TWO levels). Three-level `[[[Hinv...]]]` fails with 'invalid intro-pattern: nothing to eliminate'.
big_consT must be a rewrite (`rewrite !big_consT /=`), NOT an smt hint. Passing it to smt as `smt(big_consT ...)` does not unfold big-sums and will fail.

After `while (<invariant>); auto.` on a HOARE goal, the remaining combined base+post goal has shape: `forall &hr, (pre_cond) => (<invariant>_at_entry) /\ forall exit_vars, !guard => <invariant>_at_exit => postcondition`. Close with: `move=> &hr [-> [-> [-> H]]]; split; [by rewrite !big_nil xorw0_or_similar | move=> v1 v2 j0 Hguard [Hinv [Hj _]]; smt()]`. The two parts are: (1) base case — prove invariant holds at entry (j=0, s=[], s'=[]) using big_nil to reduce big-sums over empty lists; (2) postcondition case — prove that when guard is false (j>=N) and i0 was in range (0<=i0<N), the invariant implies the desired postcondition.

patterns.structural_description: Prove islossless (procedure terminates with probability 1). Two variants: (A) no while loop — chain of rnd predT for each sampling, call _ll for each oracle call, working backward; (B) while loop — procedure fills a log or counter via repeated sampling from a lossless distribution until a size bound is reached, standard rnd predT chain will NOT work, use while (true) (size_bound - current_size) to establish termination.
indicators: islossless in goal
pre = true, post = true, bound [=] 1%r
Variant B: program body contains a while loop that samples from a distribution and accumulates results into a list or log
typical_tactics: VARIANT A (no while loop): proc; inline *; wp
while (true) (size_bound - size current_log). auto => />; smt(dist_ll size_ge0).
patterns.common_failure: cannot_close_goal
patterns.fallback: Check all distributions are lossless. Conditional distributions (dexcepted) need special handling. If rnd predT chain is stuck, the procedure likely has a while loop — switch to Variant B.
reasoning_hints: Work backward: wp consumes deterministic steps, rnd predT consumes each sampling
For oracle calls, use call Oc_ll where Oc_ll proves the oracle is lossless
Order: call first, then wp, then rnd, then skip

WHILE LOOP CASE: If the procedure has a while loop (e.g., filling a log until size reaches n), rnd predT chain cannot handle the loop. Instead, use `while (true) (n - size log)` to reduce to two obligations: (1) loop body terminates with prob >= 1 per iteration (use auto + smt(dist_ll) to discharge), (2) variant is non-negative before the loop (use auto + smt(size_ge0)). The variant expression must decrease strictly: size grows by 1 each iteration, so n - size decreases. Key SMT hints: dist_ll proves the sampling distribution is lossless; size_ge0 proves size is non-negative ensuring the variant bound is reachable.

patterns.structural_description: Combine previously proved lemma bounds via rewrite chain. Typically the final theorem in a security proof.
indicators: All intermediate lemmas already proved

patterns.structural_description: Game-hopping proof: assemble probability bounds from intermediate lemmas. Three variants differ in how the intermediate lemmas are marshalled into the final goal. (A) `have_chain_smt` — materialise each intermediate lemma with `have h_i := <lem_i> &m.`, then close with one `smt()` call that sees all h_i. Useful when the chain combines equalities and inequalities with distances / absolute values. (B) `ler_trans_assembly` — when the final goal has shape `Pr[<G_real>] <= Pr[<G_ideal>] + <bound>`, split via `apply (ler_trans (Pr[<G_ideal>] + Pr[<G_bad> : <bad_event>]))`, discharge the first subgoal with <game_hop_lemma>, cancel the shared summand with `apply ler_add2l`, and close the residual `Pr[<bad_event>] <= <bound>` with `byphoare <bad_bound_lemma> => //`. (C) `direct_smt_with_named_lemmas` — when every intermediate result is a named local lemma in scope and the arithmetic chain is simple, a single `smt(<lemmas>)` listing them all closes the goal.

slot_semantics.<G_real>: the concrete real-game module appearing on the left of the final inequality
slot_semantics.<G_ideal>: the idealized game module appearing on the right of the final inequality
slot_semantics.<G_bad>: the instrumented game module carrying the bad-event variable (variant B only)
slot_semantics.<bad_event>: the predicate/variable expressing the bad event inside <G_bad> (variant B only)
slot_semantics.<bound>: the concrete numeric bound on the bad-event probability (variant B only)
slot_semantics.<game_hop_lemma>: in variant B, the lemma stating `Pr[<G_real>] <= Pr[<G_ideal>] + Pr[<G_bad> : <bad_event>]`
slot_semantics.<bad_bound_lemma>: in variant B, the phoare/byphoare lemma stating `Pr[<G_bad> : <bad_event>] <= <bound>`
slot_semantics.<lemmas>: space-separated list of named local lemmas to pass into `smt(...)` (variants A & C). Each lemma must already be parameterized by &m where applicable, so `smt` can see the instantiated probabilities.
slot_semantics.<lem1>: first lemma of the have-chain (variant A)
slot_semantics.<lem2>: second lemma of the have-chain (variant A)
slot_semantics.<lem3>: third lemma of the have-chain (variant A; may be absent for short chains)
slot_semantics.<lem4>: fourth lemma of the have-chain (variant A; optional)
slot_semantics.<lem5>: fifth lemma of the have-chain (variant A; optional)
slot_semantics.<lem6>: sixth lemma of the have-chain (variant A; optional)

indicators: Goal is a probability inequality `Pr[<G_real>] <= ... + ...` (possibly with distances / absolute values)
The file already declares several local lemmas bounding intermediate Pr[] expressions, each parameterized by &m
The final lemma's proof argument is short (a few tactics at most) and does not itself manipulate pRHL programs

variants.name: have_chain_smt
variants.conditions: (1) The final-lemma proof introduces 2-6 named local lemmas with `have h_i := <lem_i> &m.`, then closes with a single `smt()` or `smt(hints)` that sees every h_i; (2) no `byequiv` / `byphoare` / `ler_trans` invoked inside the final lemma; (3) intermediate results include both equalities and inequalities (possibly with distances or absolute values) that smt needs to chain after materialisation.

variants.name: ler_trans_assembly
variants.conditions: (1) The final-lemma statement has shape `Pr[<G_real>.main() @ &m : res] <= Pr[<G_ideal>.main() @ &m : res] + <bound>`; (2) the file has a <game_hop_lemma> proving `Pr[<G_real>] <= Pr[<G_ideal>] + Pr[<G_bad> : <bad_event>]`; (3) the file has a phoare / byphoare <bad_bound_lemma> bounding `Pr[<G_bad> : <bad_event>] <= <bound>`; (4) the proof uses `apply (ler_trans ...)` to split, `apply ler_add2l` to cancel the shared `Pr[<G_ideal>]` summand, and `byphoare` to discharge the residual bad-event bound.

variants.name: direct_smt_with_named_lemmas
variants.conditions: (1) Every intermediate Pr[] result is already a named local lemma in scope, parameterized by &m; (2) the arithmetic chain is simple (sequence of <= and = steps, no absolute values or distance lemmas); (3) the final-lemma proof argument is a single `smt(<lem1> <lem2> ...)` call that lists every relevant lemma; (4) no preliminary `have` steps, no `byequiv` / `byphoare` / `ler_trans`.

reasoning_hints: Use `direct_smt_with_named_lemmas` FIRST when every intermediate Pr[] fact is a named local lemma and the chain is purely algebraic — no distances, no absolute values. A single `smt(<lem1> <lem2> ...)` often closes the goal. This is the cheapest recipe to try.

Use `have_chain_smt` when `direct_smt_with_named_lemmas` times out, or when the chain involves distances / absolute values / non-linear steps. Materializing each lemma with `have h_i := <lem_i> &m.` exposes the instantiated Pr values to smt, which lets the solver chain them even when direct lemma application would miss unification.

Use `ler_trans_assembly` when: (1) the goal is exactly `Pr[<G_real>] <= Pr[<G_ideal>] + <bound>`; (2) there exist two complementary local lemmas — a game-hop bound and a phoare/byphoare bad-event bound. The `ler_trans` splits into a bound-via-bad-event step + a bad-event-probability step, `ler_add2l` cancels the shared Pr[<G_ideal>] summand, and `byphoare` discharges the residual.
For `ler_trans_assembly`: scan file-local lemmas BEFORE starting. If you can identify both a game-hop bound lemma and a phoare/byphoare bad-event bound lemma, Variant B is the right approach.
EC lemma names used: `ler_trans` (transitivity of <= on reals), `ler_add2l` (left-addition cancellation: `x + a <= x + b <=> a <= b`).
patterns.common_failure: ler_trans_wrong_intermediate
patterns.fallback: For `direct_smt_with_named_lemmas`: if smt() with all named lemmas times out, switch to `have_chain_smt` — materialize each lemma with `have h_i := <lem_i> &m.`, then `smt(h0 h1 ...)` sees them. For `have_chain_smt`: if smt() cannot close, include specific hints `smt(h0 h1 mu_bounded)`. For `ler_trans_assembly`: ensure the `ler_trans` intermediate expression matches the <game_hop_lemma> statement exactly (`Pr[<G_ideal>] + Pr[<G_bad> : <bad_event>]`). If `ler_add2l` does not apply, the goal is not in the `Pr[<G_ideal>] + X <= Pr[<G_ideal>] + Y` canonical form — rewrite it there first.

patterns.structural_description: Pure logic/set theory: prove fset equality, membership, or project-local restriction properties. No program, just set rewriting.
indicators: Goal involves fset, <restr_op>, <restr_pred>, mem_oflist, mem_iota
No Pr[] or program statements

slot_semantics.<restr_op>: a project-local set-restriction operator (e.g. restr in <program>)
slot_semantics.<restr_pred>: a project-local set-restriction predicate (e.g. is_restr in <program>)

patterns.common_failure: nothing_to_rewrite
patterns.fallback: Use have to introduce intermediate facts about membership. For negation, use negP or apply/negP.
reasoning_hints: fsetP converts set equality to pointwise membership: S1 = S2 <=> forall x, x in S1 <=> x in S2
Unfold /<restr_op>, /<restr_pred> to expose the underlying set operations
in_fsetI (intersection), in_fsetU (union), in_fset1 (singleton), mem_oflist, mem_iota for range sets

patterns.structural_description: Pure algebraic/logic identity: XOR cancellation, <op>/format roundtrip, arithmetic. No program.
indicators: Goal is a pure equality or implication
Involves +^, ||, <op>, format, ring operations

slot_semantics.<op>: a scheme-local operator whose round-trip or algebraic identity is the target of the lemma (e.g. an encoding/decoding op or a bit-level helper)

patterns.structural_description: Trivial program equivalence: apply an existing equiv lemma directly.
indicators: Goal is Pr[G1]=Pr[G2]
An equiv lemma for exactly this pair already exists

patterns.structural_description: <op_dec> inverts <op_enc>: show <op_dec>(<op_enc>(m))=m for an encryption or commitment scheme. When the goal is a single-program probability bound (the game always succeeds), open with a single-sided probabilistic tactic (byphoare), NOT a two-sided relational tactic — relational tactics require a matching right-side program that does not exist for single-program goals. After opening, expand the game body and the concrete scheme procedures, then close with auto. If auto does not close it, supply explicit algebraic lemma hints for key-injectivity, additive-cancellation (e.g., XOR cancellation), and distribution losslessness.
indicators: hoare or byphoare goal
correctness game (encrypt then decrypt)
Pr[<correctness_game>.main(m) @ &m : res] = 1%r — single-program probability, use byphoare not byequiv

slot_semantics.<correctness_game>: the correctness game module applied to the scheme
slot_semantics.<op_enc>: the encrypt procedure name on the scheme
slot_semantics.<op_dec>: the decrypt procedure name on the scheme
slot_semantics.<op_keygen>: the keygen procedure name on the scheme
slot_semantics.<hash_op>: the hash/random-oracle op used for masking in the scheme
slot_semantics.<d_ptxt>: the plaintext distribution (appearing in the correctness game)
slot_semantics.<trapdoor_lemma>: lemma stating fi sk (f pk r) = r under the key predicate

reasoning_hints: CRITICAL: Pr[<correctness_game>.main(m) @ &m : res] = 1%r is a single-program probability bound — open with byphoare, NOT byequiv. byequiv is for two-program relational reasoning and will require constructing a matching right-side program that does not exist here.

After byphoare=> //, proc expands the game and inline expands the concrete scheme modules. The goal reduces to showing the algebraic identity <op_dec>(<op_enc>(m)) = m holds for all sampled randomness.

For XOR-based <hash_op> encryption (e.g., Bellare-Rogaway 1993 style): <op_enc> computes (trapdoor_function r, <hash_op>(r) XOR m); <op_dec> inverts the trapdoor to recover r, looks up <hash_op>(r), and unmasks. The key algebraic identity is <hash_op>(r) XOR (<hash_op>(r) XOR m) = m, captured by addpK.
Losslessness of the plaintext distribution (<ll_ptxt>) is needed to discharge the distribution weight side condition.

LRO inline order (critical): for LRO-based schemes, use selective inline naming the scheme procedures (<op_keygen>, <op_enc>, <op_dec>) but NOT LRO.o. After wp., inline LRO.o separately. Using inline * prematurely expands all oracle calls, introducing redundant variables (x, x0, r1, r2 from double LRO.o expansion) that complicate the ambient goal without benefit.

After `wp. inline LRO.o. wp; auto => />.`, the goal reduces to a three-level nested forall over distribution weight obligations (<d_keys>, <d_rand>, <d_ptxt>). Discharge each weight with the corresponding losslessness lemma (<ll_keys>, <ll_rand>, <ll_ptxt>), then case-split on LRO.m membership: use mem_set for the contradiction branch (r notin m.[r<-v] is always false) and get_set_sameE for the positive branch (resolve double-set lookup), then additive cancellation.
smt_hints: <trapdoor_lemma> (trapdoor function injectivity / one-way inverse: fi sk (f pk r) = r) — materialize via `have HfK := <trapdoor_lemma> pk sk r Hkeys; rewrite HfK` (direct rewrite with wildcards fails: too many arguments)
addpK (XOR cancellation: x +^ (x +^ m) = m) — apply via `rewrite get_set_sameE /= -addA addKp`
patterns.common_failure: starting with byequiv on a single-program Pr[...]=1%r goal — byequiv requires a two-sided relational judgment and will fail or produce unresolvable sub-goals
patterns.fallback: NOTE: smt() may be unavailable in some sandbox environments — do NOT use `auto with smt(...)` blindly. For LRO-based schemes: use selective inline (scheme procedures only, NOT inline *), then wp, then inline LRO.o, then wp; auto => />. Then manually: (1) move=> &hr, (2) split; first exact <ll_keys>, (3) move=> _ [pk sk] /= Hkeys _; split; first exact <ll_rand>, (4) move=> _ r _ _; split; first exact <ll_ptxt>, (5) move=> _ h1 _ _; have HfK := <trapdoor_lemma> pk sk r Hkeys; rewrite HfK, (6) split — case-split on r \in LRO.m, (7) for r-notin branch: contradiction via mem_set, (8) for r-in branch: get_set_sameE then additive cancellation.

patterns.structural_description: Prove distribution uniformity or bijection-induced distribution equality via bypr: reduce the equiv goal `<G_real> ~ <G_ideal> : true ==> res{1} = <inverse_fn> res{2}` to showing Pr[<G_real> outputs a] = Pr[<G_ideal> outputs <inverse_fn> a] for all a. This leverages explicit point-probability lemmas <pr_closed_real> / <pr_closed_ideal> on both sides and avoids the bijection measure-equality obligation that arises in the rnd-coupling approach.

slot_semantics.<G_real>: one of the two samplers (appears on the left of the equiv)
slot_semantics.<G_ideal>: the other sampler (appears on the right of the equiv)
slot_semantics.<pr_closed_real>: explicit point-probability lemma for <G_real> (Pr[<G_real> = a] = expr)
slot_semantics.<pr_closed_ideal>: explicit point-probability lemma for <G_ideal> (Pr[<G_ideal> = a] = expr)
slot_semantics.<fwd_fn>: forward bijection argument mapping <G_real>'s output domain to <G_ideal>'s
slot_semantics.<inverse_fn>: inverse bijection argument; the RHS of the postcondition is <inverse_fn> res{2}
slot_semantics.<domain_iff>: the hypothesis of the form `forall i, in_domain i <=> in_domain (<fwd_fn> i)`
slot_semantics.<inverse_spec>: the hypothesis `forall i, in_domain i => <fwd_fn>(<inverse_fn> i)=i /\ <inverse_fn>(<fwd_fn> i)=i`

indicators: equiv goal about output distributions
Need to show same distribution for different inputs or bijection-related inputs
Explicit point-probability lemmas (<pr_closed_real> / <pr_closed_ideal>, or byphoare-proved values) are available for both programs
The rnd bijection approach would require proving mu1 D x = mu1 D (<inverse_fn> x) as a sub-obligation
reasoning_hints: bypr reduces an equiv goal `<G_real> ~ <G_ideal> : true ==> res{1} = <inverse_fn> res{2}` to showing: for all a, Pr[<G_real> = a] = Pr[<G_ideal> = <inverse_fn> a]. Each side is then computed directly via an explicit probability lemma (<pr_closed_real>, <pr_closed_ideal>).

PREFER bypr over proc + transitivity + rnd bijection when explicit point-probability lemmas exist for both programs. The rnd approach requires proving mu1 D x = mu1 D (<inverse_fn> x) as an inline obligation, which needs additional lemmas (dinter1E) and range hypotheses about <inverse_fn>. bypr bypasses this by reducing to arithmetic on the explicit probability values.

Event transformation: after bypr, the right-side event `res{2} = a` may need to be transformed to `res{2} = <inverse_fn> (<fwd_fn> a)` or similar. Use <domain_iff> (the bijection range hypothesis) and <inverse_spec> to relate the two forms.

For the backward event direction (<inverse_fn> side): derive `in_domain a` from `in_domain (<fwd_fn> a)` using the backward iff direction. Correct: `have Ha: <domain> by (move: Hr; rewrite -<domain_iff>). have [_ ->] := <inverse_spec> a Ha.` See ec_syntax iff_hypothesis_backward_direction.
patterns.common_failure: Using `rewrite <domain_iff>` instead of `rewrite -<domain_iff>` when extracting A from the hypothesis `A <=> B` with B in context. See ec_syntax iff_hypothesis_backward_direction for the full pattern.
patterns.fallback: If bypr produces a goal that is hard to close via point-probability arithmetic, fall back to proc + transitivity{1} + rnd bijection. If the rnd approach produces a mu1 equality sub-obligation, close it with `rewrite !dinter1E Hx Hy` (see ec_syntax dinter1E_measure_equality) after establishing both endpoints are in range.

patterns.structural_description: Apply an abstract lemma from a cloned theory (e.g., DLog.dlog_standard_reduction, Ad1.pr_abs). The proof is essentially instantiating the abstract result.
indicators: Goal matches an abstract lemma signature
Proof is apply or have := lemma

patterns.structural_description: Prove Pr[game]=1 (completeness) for a sigma protocol. The game always succeeds when the witness <witness> satisfies the relation <relation>. The game samples a masking term and a <chal> uniformly, then checks a verification equation; the check always passes because the group exponent arithmetic and losslessness of the distributions are sufficient to close the ambient goal.
indicators: Completeness or correctness game for a sigma protocol
Game contains two or more samplings (e.g., masking term and <chal>)
Verification equation holds when the witness <witness> satisfies the relation <relation>

slot_semantics.<relation>: the sigma-protocol relation predicate (often an abbreviation in the .ec file, e.g. R_DL)
slot_semantics.<witness>: the witness component of the relation precondition pair passed to byphoare
slot_semantics.<witness_pair>: the full (statement, witness) pair used as the byphoare precondition
slot_semantics.<chal>: the challenge variable sampled by the game
slot_semantics.<dist>: the distribution sampled at each rnd predT step

reasoning_hints: Two rnd predT applications (one per sampling) generate a doubly-nested ambient goal: outer `weight <dist> = 1%r &&` conjoined with inner `forall v0, v0 \in <dist> => predT v0 <=> weight <dist> = 1%r && forall v1, v1 \in <dist> => predT v1 <=> verification_equation`.
Do NOT manually decompose this nested structure with split/apply <ll_lemma> — this is laborious and fragile. Delegate the entire closure to smt.

The simplifier `/=` (in `move=> &hr /=`) is required before any `->` destructuring when the ambient goal precondition contains a pair equality `(a, b) = (c, d)`. Without `/=`, the pair equality is not flattened into `a = c /\ b = d`, so `-> ->` silently fails to substitute.
<ll_lemma>: establishes weight <dist> = 1%r (losslessness of the <chal>/masking distribution).
<fu_lemma>: establishes full support — every element is in the distribution's support.
expD / expM: group exponent arithmetic laws (e.g., g^a * g^b = g^(a+b), (g^a)^b = g^(a*b)) needed to verify the protocol equation.

If the relation <relation> is defined as an abbreviation, do NOT try `rewrite /<relation> in Hyp` to unfold it before smt — the rewrite may have no effect if <relation> is a notation. The SMT backend resolves the relation directly from its definition.
patterns.common_failure: tactic_accepted_wrong_effect — `move=> &hr [[-> ->] _]` is accepted but does not substitute the memory-indexed variables from the pair equality (e.g., `x{hr}` and `w{hr}` persist). Root cause: EasyCrypt does not split a pair equality `(a, b) = (c, d)` into rewrite hypotheses without the `/=` simplifier.
patterns.fallback: Use `move=> &hr /=` instead of `move=> &hr [[-> ->] _]`. The `/=` simplifier flattens the pair equality into a propositional conjunction, after which `move=> [-> ->]` works. Then close the entire goal with `smt(<ll_lemma> <fu_lemma> expD expM)` — no manual split/apply needed.

patterns.structural_description: Prove extraction from two accepting transcripts (sigma protocol special soundness). Use byphoare with argument precondition encoding the two transcripts. After opening and fully expanding the procedure body, use auto => /> (NOT wp; skip) to reduce the boolean post-condition — this avoids a stuck state with boolean && conjunctions that standard tactics cannot decompose. Then close the remaining propositional goal by splitting into the witness equation and the hypothesis equations, using loge algebra and field for discrete log extraction.
indicators: Two transcripts with different challenges
Extract witness via division in exponent
Boolean postcondition with <relation>, msg, and hypothesis equalities after proc; inline *

slot_semantics.<relation>: the sigma-protocol relation predicate (often an abbreviation in the .ec file, e.g. R_DL)
slot_semantics.<transcript_args>: the argument tuple encoding both transcripts, e.g. (h, msg, ch, r, ch', r')

reasoning_hints: CRITICAL: Use auto => /> instead of wp; skip after proc; inline *. The wp; skip tactic produces a goal with boolean `&&` conjunctions from program-level if-then-else residuals. Tactics like split, do !split, apply/andP, rewrite ... /= all silently have no effect on boolean `&&`.

auto => /> automatically reduces the boolean postcondition, discharges if-then-else branches, and introduces hypotheses, giving a clean propositional goal: ch <> ch' => g^r = msg * h^ch => g^r' = msg * h^ch' => <relation> h ((r - r') / (ch - ch')) /\ msg * h^ch = g^r /\ msg * h^ch' = g^r'.

After auto => />, use move=> Hneq Hr Hr' to name the three hypotheses, then split the triple conjunction manually into (1) the <relation> witness goal, (2) the eq_sym Hr goal, (3) the eq_sym Hr' goal.

For the witness goal: rewrite /<relation> followed by rewrite log_bij loggK reduces the group equality to an exponent equality. Then prove Hr_l / Hr'_l expressing responses as linear functions of loge h.

ORDERING CRITICAL: substitute Hr_l and Hr'_l with 'rewrite Hr_l Hr'_l.' BEFORE calling 'field.'. This makes field produce denominator ch - ch' (not (-ch') + ch), enabling 'rewrite GP.ZModE.ZModpRing.subr_eq0; exact Hneq.' to close the side condition.
For the Hr / Hr' goals: 'by rewrite eq_sym Hr.' and 'by rewrite eq_sym Hr'.' close these directly.
patterns.common_failure: wp_skip_produces_boolean_conjunction — after proc; inline *, using wp; skip produces a goal with boolean `&&` conjunctions. Tactics split, do !split, apply/andP; split, rewrite Hkey (eq_sym Hr) (eq_sym Hr') Hneq /= all silently accept but have no effect. Scrapping wp; skip and using auto => /> instead is the fix, not debugging the boolean split.
patterns.fallback: Undo to proc; inline * and replace wp; skip with auto => />. This is a complete substitution. After auto => />, the goal is propositional and standard split/exact/rewrite tactics apply. If field produces (-ch') + ch instead of ch - ch', the fix is to rewrite Hr_l Hr'_l into the goal BEFORE calling field.

patterns.structural_description: Prove a bound of the shape `|Pr[<M1>(A).main : <E1>] - Pr[<M2>(A).main : <E2>]| <= Pr[<M2>(A).main : <bad_event>]` by applying an abstract up-to-bad lemma whose statement has that shape. The lemma takes the two games, a negated-adversary functor <neg_adv>, and explicit predicate/event parameters; after `apply`, the remaining subgoals concern the negated-adversary equivalence and complement probabilities for events on each game.
indicators: Goal has shape `|Pr[<M1>(A).main : <E1>] - Pr[<M2>(A).main : <E2>]| <= Pr[<M2>(A).main : <bad_event>]`
Proof context defines or imports a negated-adversary functor (a module type that wraps an adversary oracle)
File declares schematic games <M1>/<M2> plus adversary A and exposes an abstract reusable up-to-bad lemma

slot_semantics.<abs_lemma>: the abstract up-to-bad lemma being applied (takes M1 M2 N A predicates, yields the absolute-difference bound)
slot_semantics.<eqN1>: equiv relating <M1>(<neg_adv>(A)) to <M1>(A) with complemented <E1> post
slot_semantics.<eqN2>: equiv relating <M2>(<neg_adv>(A)) to <M2>(A) with complemented <E2> post and preserved <bad_event>
slot_semantics.<neg_adv>: the negated-adversary functor (module type wrapping A's oracles)

patterns.common_failure: congr_shape_mismatch: `rewrite Pr[mu_not]; congr; byphoare <llM>.` assumes both sides already have the shape `1 - Pr[:E]`. If the subtraction is flipped so one side reads `Pr[:E] = 1 - Pr[:!E]`, congr will refuse to unify. Secondary failure: using `smt(<llM>)` for a `weight d = 1%r` sub-goal — smt may silently stall where `exact <llM>` succeeds immediately.
patterns.fallback: When `congr` refuses to unify because the subtraction polarity is flipped, introduce the islossless fact as a named hypothesis: `have ll : Pr[<M>(A).main(vx) @ &m : true] = 1%r` proved `by byphoare (_: _ ==> true)=> //; exact <llM>.`, then `rewrite Pr[mu_not] ll /#` to close the arithmetic residual. Prefer `exact <llM>` (not `smt(<llM>)`) for lossless weight obligations.
reasoning_hints: Structure: `apply <abs_lemma>` yields five follow-up obligations — the two negated-adversary equivalences (closed with `byequiv <eqN1>` / `byequiv <eqN2>`), the two complement-probability identities for <E1> / <E2> on <M1>(A) / <M2>(A), and the preservation of <bad_event> under negation of the adversary.

The complement-probability identities are proved by: (a) rewriting the negated-adversary probability as the complement of the plain-adversary probability using <eqN1>/<eqN2>, then (b) invoking `rewrite Pr[mu_not]` together with an islossless fact `Pr[<M>(A).main(vx) @ &m : true] = 1%r` (proved by `byphoare + exact <llM>`), then `ring` + `smt()`.

Scoping: the abstract lemma's predicate arguments <E1>, <E2>, <bad_event> are higher-order functions over `glob <M>` / `glob A` / `res`. Pass them as-is to `apply`; do not expand the predicate body inline.

patterns.structural_description: Prove equiv via transitivity: show G1 ~ G_mid and G_mid ~ G2. Used when direct equiv is hard due to structural mismatch between two game variants. An explicit postcondition captures only the state variables that differ between the two game variants; a transitivity step then introduces an intermediate program by structural insertion. Both equiv conditions (left and right) must be stated around the intermediate body.
indicators: byequiv with transitivity{1}
Intermediate game or module
Two game variants that share most state but differ in a few variables (e.g., log, random oracle map)
Custom byequiv postcondition with explicit state variable equalities
reasoning_hints: The byequiv postcondition must capture EXACTLY the state variables that differ between the two game variants — not the full state. Over-specifying causes byequiv to fail; under-specifying leaves the final rewrite unsolvable.
transitivity{1} introduces an intermediate program body inline. The two precondition/postcondition pairs specify the two hops: left is G1 ~ G_mid, right is G_mid ~ G2.
The intermediate body is typically a re-ordered or re-initialized version of one of the games (e.g., init oracles before running adversary, or resample before sampling).
patterns.common_failure: wrong postcondition variables in byequiv — if too few variables are captured, the final goal cannot be closed; if too many, byequiv rejects
patterns.fallback: Read the final goal shape after byequiv to identify which variables appear unrelated; adjust the postcondition to include exactly those variables.

patterns.structural_description: Losslessness subgoal for an abstract module call: the goal is islossless A(O).<adv_proc> (or phoare[A(O).<adv_proc> : true ==> true] = 1%r) where A is an abstract adversary with a section axiom like 'forall O, islossless O.<oracle_proc> => islossless A(O).<adv_proc>'. The oracle O is a concrete module (e.g., <concrete_oracle>).
indicators: Goal is phoare[A(O).<adv_proc> : true ==> true] = 1%r
A is an abstract module (declared in a section)
There is a section axiom: forall (O <: ORC{-A}), islossless O.<oracle_proc> => islossless A(O).<adv_proc>
O is a concrete oracle module

slot_semantics.<adv_proc>: the adversary procedure whose losslessness we are trying to prove (e.g. choose, guess)
slot_semantics.<oracle_proc>: the oracle procedure referenced in the section losslessness axiom
slot_semantics.<concrete_oracle>: the concrete oracle module being passed as O (e.g. CCA(Scheme,A).O)
slot_semantics.<ll_axiom>: the section losslessness axiom name bound to <adv_proc>

patterns.common_failure: apply_ll_axiom_module_inference
patterns.fallback: Do NOT try 'apply <ll_axiom>' directly on the islossless goal -- EasyCrypt cannot infer the module argument O and gives 'cannot infer module arguments'. Do NOT try 'proc' without an invariant -- gives 'abstract, provide invariant'. The correct tactic is 'proc (true)' which generates 4 subgoals. The section axiom subgoal (subgoal 3) matches the axiom exactly, so 'exact <ll_axiom>' works there.
reasoning_hints: proc (true) for abstract modules in phoare generates 4 subgoals: pre-preservation, post-preservation, losslessness axiom, oracle losslessness
The invariant 'true' is simplest -- subgoals 1 and 2 become 'forall _, true => true' which is trivial
Subgoal 3 is in the EXACT form of the section axiom (universally quantified over O), so exact/apply works
Subgoal 4 is islossless for the concrete oracle, which islossless handles automatically

patterns.structural_description: Pure logic: prove a boolean property by contradiction using an inductive predicate. A negation-of-inductive-predicate hypothesis is in context. The proof case-splits on the target property; in the failing branch, the inductive predicate's constructor is applied to build a witness; the contradiction with the negation hypothesis closes the goal.
indicators: Goal is a boolean property (uniqueness, disjointness, membership condition)
Context contains a negation of an inductively-defined predicate as hypothesis
The inductive predicate has a constructor whose precondition is the negation of the goal property
patterns.common_failure: constructor_used_as_view: writing `/Constructor` directly inside the case expression (e.g., `case: (cond) => // /Constructor.`) fails because constructors are not boolean rewriting lemmas and cannot be used as SSReflect views. Also: `apply absurd` is not a valid EasyCrypt tactic; `smt(Constructor)` may fail if the SMT backend is unavailable.
patterns.fallback: Use an explicit `have: IndPred args by apply/Constructor.` to construct the inductive witness, then `by move: h_neg.` to close by contradiction. The `have:` + `move:` two-step is the canonical EC idiom for this shape of contradiction.
reasoning_hints: Case-splitting on the goal property yields two branches: the trivial branch (property holds, `//` closes it) and the failing branch (h_failing_case: property is false)
In the failing branch, the inductive predicate's constructor fires precisely when the goal property fails — use `apply/Constructor` to build the witness
Moving the negation hypothesis (move: h_neg.) unifies it with the just-constructed witness and closes the goal by False elimination

patterns.structural_description: Pure logic: prove that the negation of an inductive predicate is equivalent to a conjunction of conditions, one per constructor. Each constructor's firing condition is the negation of exactly one conjunct. Forward direction: from the negation hypothesis, derive each conjunct by case-splitting on the condition and deriving a contradiction via the corresponding constructor. Backward direction: from the conjunction, refute each constructor by showing one of its preconditions is denied by the conjunction.
indicators: Goal is `! IndPred args <=> (cond1 /\ cond2 /\ ...)`
The inductive predicate has named constructors, each requiring conditions that correspond to negating one conjunct
Forward direction must derive each conjunct from the negation of the predicate
Backward direction must prove the negation from the conjunction
typical_tactics: (prove cond1 by contradiction) case: (cond1_expr) => //. move=> h_fail. apply h_neg. apply/Ctor1.
(prove cond2 by contradiction) move=> arg. case: (cond2_expr arg) => //=. move=> h_mem. apply/negP => h_other. apply h_neg. exact (Ctor2 arg h_mem h_other).
(backward) move=> [h_cond1 h_cond2]. apply/negP => h_bad. case: h_bad.
(Ctor2 branch) move=> arg h_mem h_other. move: (h_cond2 arg). by rewrite h_mem h_other.
reasoning_hints: Forward: for each conjunct cond_i, case-split on cond_i_expr; the trivially-true branch is closed by `//`; in the failing branch, the corresponding constructor's precondition is satisfied — apply the negation hypothesis then the constructor

Two equivalent idioms for the contradiction step: (A) `apply h_neg; apply/Constructor` — apply h_neg turns goal to IndPred, then apply Constructor proves it; (B) `exact (Constructor arg1 arg2 ...)` for direct multi-argument instantiation

Backward: always starts with `apply/negP => h_bad; case: h_bad.` — see ec_syntax negP_inductive_negation. Each constructor branch is closed by showing one of its preconditions is contradicted by a conjunct

NEVER use `smt(Constructor)` to prove an inductive predicate goal — SMT backends cannot reason about EC inductive constructors and will fail if why3 is unavailable. Use `apply/Constructor` or `exact (Constructor args)` directly
patterns.common_failure: constructor_as_view_or_smt: (1) writing `/Constructor` directly in a case expression as an SSReflect view — constructors are not rewriting lemmas; (2) using `smt(Constructor)` — unreliable and fails when why3 is unavailable; (3) using `elim: h_bad.` instead of `case: h_bad.` in the backward direction — see ec_syntax case_vs_elim_inductive; (4) mixing intro patterns between forward and backward subgoals after a complex split
patterns.fallback: If stuck on a constructor branch in the forward direction: use `have: IndPred args by apply/Constructor.` + `by move: h_neg.` (two-step form). For multi-argument constructors, `exact (Constructor arg1 arg2 ...)` explicitly instantiates all arguments. See ec_syntax negP_inductive_negation for backward direction details and inductive_negation_contradiction pattern for individual contradiction steps.

patterns.structural_description: Two games <G_lazy> and <G_eager> relate lazy sampling (an oracle <oracle_lazy> generates randomness on-demand, updating <accum_state> on each call) to eager sampling (all randomness is batch-resampled at game end via a dedicated <resample_proc> procedure). Direct program equivalence fails because the structures differ: <G_lazy> samples inside each oracle call, while <G_eager> runs <resample_proc> after the adversary finishes. The equivalence uses a transitivity argument through an intermediate program that runs init; adversary; <resample_proc>, reducing the obligation to an eager/lazy permutation of independent sampling operations on <accum_state>.
indicators: Left game calls a lazy oracle <oracle_lazy> that samples fresh randomness on each call and updates <accum_state> (e.g., a query log and/or a memoization map)
Right game <G_eager> calls the same oracle but runs a batch <resample_proc> after the adversary finishes
Goal shape: Pr[<G_lazy> @ &m : <event>] = Pr[<G_eager> @ &m : <event>]
After byequiv + proc, the two programs differ in length because the right side runs <resample_proc> at the end
<event> is over <accum_state> (e.g., a bad predicate over a log and a map)

slot_semantics.<G_lazy>: the lazy-sampling game module (instantiated with the lazy oracle)
slot_semantics.<G_eager>: the eager-sampling game module (adds batch resample after the adversary, OR simply adds a logging side-effect while keeping the call structure identical)
slot_semantics.<oracle_lazy>: the lazy oracle module whose per-call body samples and accumulates state
slot_semantics.<resample_proc>: the batch eager-resample procedure run at game end (typically a counter-bounded while loop rebuilding the log) — only relevant for the transitivity variant
slot_semantics.<accum_state>: the shared variables the two games must agree on as postcondition of byequiv (e.g., a query log and a memoization map) — appears in the top-level byequiv post
slot_semantics.<event>: the predicate evaluated over <accum_state> on both sides
slot_semantics.<state_eq_pre>: the intermediate equality used as invariant when coupling <G_lazy> to the intermediate program (transitivity variant)
slot_semantics.<eager_body_ll>: losslessness lemma for <oracle_lazy>'s per-call body (needed when reducing under `eager`)
slot_semantics.<call_inv>: the state invariant preserved by the oracle across the per-call boundary; used as the sole argument of `call (_: <call_inv>)` in the direct variant when both games share identical oracle-call sequences and differ only by a logging-style side-effect (direct variant)
slot_semantics.<intermediate_block>: the literal statement block inserted by `transitivity{1} { ... }`, matching <G_eager>'s structure: init subroutines, adversary call, then <resample_proc> (transitivity variant)

variants.name: direct_byequiv_preserved_state
