variants.conditions: Both <G_lazy> and <G_eager> share the identical top-level call structure (same sequence of oracle / adversary calls, same number of statements on each side). <G_eager> differs from <G_lazy> only by an extra logging side-effect inside the oracle (e.g., pushing the sampled value into a seed log). The goal shape is `Pr[<G_lazy> @ &m : res] = Pr[<G_eager> @ &m : res]` with res-only event — not a `Bad`-predicate event. A single `call (_: <call_inv>)` + `sim` on each per-oracle subgoal suffices.

variants.name: transitivity_through_resample
variants.conditions: <G_eager> runs <resample_proc> AFTER the adversary, so the two top-level programs differ in length (the right side has extra statements after the adversary call). The goal shape is `Pr[<G_lazy> @ &m : <event>] = Pr[<G_eager> @ &m : <event>]` where <event> is a predicate over <accum_state> (typically a `Bad` predicate over a log and a map). Requires splitting the obligation via `transitivity{1}` with an intermediate program that mimics <G_eager>'s structure, then discharging the remaining eager obligation via the `eager` tactic family.

patterns.common_failure: stuck_on_eager_obligation: after transitivity{1} and dispatching obligations 1-3, the eager obligation (shape `eager[ <resample_proc>;, <oracle_lazy> ~ <oracle_lazy>, <resample_proc>; ]`) requires specialized reasoning about permuting independent samples. Standard tactics (sim, proc, auto, rnd without explicit bijection, call) do not close it. Two distinct failure modes: (A) swap_not_independent — <oracle_lazy> has a while loop whose guard reads the sampled variable (rejection-sampling). Additional swap configurations cannot succeed. (B) eager_proc_failed — <resample_proc> itself contains a while loop, so `eager proc` and `eager proc; wp.` both fail; the fix requires inlining both programs and handling the loop directly. Diagnose by checking WHICH program has the while loop before choosing a fix strategy.
patterns.fallback: If the eager tactic family is unavailable or fails: (1) Restate the lemma to avoid the lazy-to-eager equality — bound Pr[<G_lazy>] by Pr[<G_eager>] + Pr[<event>] via `have` + up-to-bad instead of equality. (2) Seq-based coupling: split at each oracle-call boundary with `seq` and show each call produces the same distribution independently using `rnd` / `auto`. (3) Express <resample_proc>'s distribution as a bijection on the full <accum_state> vector and discharge with `rnd` + explicit bijection. (4) When <oracle_lazy> uses rejection sampling and swap fails, prove the lazy-eager equivalence for <oracle_lazy> in isolation as a standalone sub-lemma (`eager [<resample_proc> : <oracle_lazy>] => //`) before composing. (5) When `eager proc` itself fails and <resample_proc> contains a while loop: use `inline *; wp.` to expose raw statements from both <resample_proc> and <oracle_lazy>, then handle the loop with `splitwhile` / `while`, and close the oracle-body portion with `rnd` coupling. If a branch has 0 probability (e.g., a freshly sampled element is not in <accum_state> by a uniqueness/uniq invariant), use `rcondf` with that 0-probability argument (typically discharged via `rnd; wp; auto` + a losslessness lemma for the sampled distribution).
reasoning_hints: Key insight: a lazy oracle that accumulates state on each call is equivalent to an eager version that batch-resamples at the end because sampling is i.i.d. and independent samples can be permuted.

EasyCrypt's eager tactic family (`eager`, `eager_seq`, `eager_if`) discharges the permutation obligation — `eager [ <resample_proc> : <oracle_lazy> ] ...` proves moving <resample_proc> from after to before each oracle call preserves the joint distribution.
The transitivity intermediate must match <G_eager>'s structure exactly: run init; adversary; <resample_proc> (in that order).

Obligation 3 (lazy-to-intermediate) is typically easy: the two programs are identical except for the extra <resample_proc> at the end; use `seq` to split at the adversary-call boundary, then `sim` or `auto`.

Obligation 4 (eager) is the hard part; when the standard `eager proc` flow fails, diagnose whether the while loop lives in <oracle_lazy> (rejection sampling — do NOT retry swap) or in <resample_proc> (eager_proc_failed — inline both and handle the loop directly).
If no eager tactic applies, seq-based coupling (split at each oracle-call boundary; prove per-call distribution equality) is the next fallback.

When <resample_proc> contains a counter-bounded while loop that rebuilds a log, `eager proc` cannot decompose it automatically. After `inline *; wp.`, use `splitwhile` / `eager_while` / direct loop tactics. A probability-0 argument (e.g., a freshly sampled element is not in <accum_state> by a uniq invariant) can enable `rcondf` to eliminate an unreachable branch.

patterns.structural_description: Prove a probability goal for a complex sampler <G_real> by reducing to a simpler sampler <G_ideal> via a parametric equiv lemma <pr_lemma> instantiated with identity functions. A parametric equiv lemma relates two samplers where a bijection function argument maps the left output to the right output. When both samplers produce the same distribution (the bijection is trivially the identity), instantiate both function arguments with the identity function. The equiv then asserts that both programs produce equal outputs, giving a direct probability equality. Close the goal by rewriting with the known closed-form lemma <pr_closed> for the simpler sampler. This avoids bypr case-analysis and the measure-equality obligation from transitivity+rnd.

slot_semantics.<G_real>: the complex sampler whose probability is being computed (target of the Pr goal)
slot_semantics.<G_ideal>: the simpler sampler whose probability is already known in closed form
slot_semantics.<pr_lemma>: the parametric equiv lemma `equiv [<G_ideal> ~ <G_real> : true ==> res{1} = <inverse_fn> res{2}]` with two bijection arguments
slot_semantics.<pr_closed>: the closed-form probability lemma for <G_ideal> (e.g. prDX k &m : Pr[<G_ideal>() @ &m : res = k] = <closed_form>)
slot_semantics.<inverse_fn>: the inverse bijection argument of <pr_lemma> (both sides are instantiated with `fun x => x` here)

indicators: Parametric equiv lemma <pr_lemma> with two function arguments (f and <inverse_fn>) exists in scope
Both programs produce the same distribution (bijection is the identity)
A closed-form probability lemma <pr_closed> is proved for the simpler program <G_ideal>
Goal is Pr[<G_real> @ &m : res = k] = <closed_form>
reasoning_hints: When both bijection arguments are the identity, the postcondition `res{1} = <inverse_fn> res{2}` simplifies to `res{1} = res{2}`, which is the self-coupling needed to relate the two Pr terms.

This approach is strictly simpler than bypr_uniformity (no per-value case analysis, no domain sub-goals) or transitivity+rnd (no measure-equality obligation) when the bijection is trivially the identity.

Use a named `have H` (not `have ->`) so the equality direction can be controlled via `rewrite -H`. byequiv proves Pr[<G_ideal>...] = Pr[<G_real>...] (left~right order in <pr_lemma>); `rewrite -H` replaces Pr[<G_real>...] in the goal with Pr[<G_ideal>...]. See ec_syntax byequiv_direction_have_naming.
patterns.common_failure: direction_mismatch_have_arrow: using `have -> : Pr[<G_real>...] = Pr[<G_ideal>...]` forces the byequiv subgoal to require the equiv in the reverse direction, which fails or is immediately undone. Fix: use `have H: Pr[<G_ideal>...] = Pr[<G_real>...]` (matching <pr_lemma>'s left~right program order), then `rewrite -H`. Additionally, after `rewrite -H`, use `apply <pr_closed>` to close the goal — NOT `exact (<pr_closed> k &m)`. `exact` requires the term to match the goal type exactly (including all implicit arguments); if there is any type mismatch, EC accepts the tactic silently but makes no progress. `apply <pr_closed>` uses unification and handles implicit argument instantiation automatically.
patterns.fallback: If identity instantiation fails (precondition side-goals are not closed by `//`), the bijection is not trivially the identity — use bypr_uniformity (P13) with explicit <pr_closed> lemmas for both programs, or R10 (transitivity with bridge lemmas for rejection-sampling programs).

patterns.structural_description: Equiv goal where the postcondition relates the two outputs through a bijection or mapping function (e.g., res{1} = <bij>(res{2}) or res{1} = <bij_inv> res{2}), and both programs <sampler_l> and <sampler_r> draw from related distributions.
indicators: equiv [A ~ B : pre ==> res{1} = <bij>(res{2})]
<bij> is a bijection between the output domains
A and B each draw from distributions that are equal up to <bij>
existing equiv lemmas show A ~ C and B ~ C for some intermediate C

slot_semantics.<bij>: the forward bijection relating the two outputs
slot_semantics.<dist_eq_lemma>: an existing equiv lemma witnessing distributional equality of <sampler_l> and <sampler_r>

reasoning_hints: KEY INSIGHT: The intermediate program is one of the ORIGINAL programs (<sampler_l> itself), NOT a third bridging program. The split is on the POSTCONDITION, not the program.
Transitivity decomposes the bijection postcondition into: (1) self-equivalence of <sampler_l> with the mapping as postcondition, and (2) distributional equality of <sampler_l> and <sampler_r>.

For the self-equivalence subgoal (<sampler_l> ~ <sampler_l> with bijection), use `proc; rnd <bij> <bij_inv>` or `proc; rnd (fun x => <bij> x) (fun x => <bij_inv> x)` to couple the two copies via the bijection.
For the distributional equality subgoal (<sampler_l> ~ <sampler_r> with ={res}), look for existing equiv lemmas (<dist_eq_lemma>) that show the two sampling procedures produce the same distribution.
Do NOT look for a third intermediate program from a clone — the clone's SampleDirect/SampleWithTest procedures are for the distributional equality part, not the transitivity intermediate.
If the file has clone-provided equiv lemmas (e.g., from WhileSamplingFixedTest), use them to close the distributional equality subgoal, NOT as the transitivity intermediate.
patterns.common_failure: Using a clone's intermediate program (e.g., SampleDirect) as the transitivity target instead of one of the original programs. This creates overly complex subgoals because the clone programs have different internal structure.
patterns.fallback: If self-transitivity doesn't work, try bypr (res{1}) (<bij_inv> res{2}) to reduce to probability equality, then prove each side separately using byphoare or existing probability lemmas.

patterns.structural_description: Embed <G_real> (an IND chosen-plaintext encryption game) into <assumption_game> (the DDH0 experiment) by byequiv. Both programs draw the same group elements but in a different order. The <assumption_game> ciphertext component is the base raised to the product of two exponents (<sk_sample> times <enc_rand>), not a fresh independent draw. After swap alignment all random draws couple via identity — no extra-mask bijection or reparameterization is needed. The only non-trivial step is closing the group exponent algebra: base raised to exponent A then to exponent B equals base raised to the product A*B.

slot_semantics.<G_real>: the concrete IND chosen-plaintext game module (left of the equiv)
slot_semantics.<assumption_game>: the DDH0 experiment game module (right of the equiv)
slot_semantics.<reduction>: the constructed reduction module wrapping <A> to attack <assumption_game>

indicators: Goal is Pr[<G_real> @ &m : res] = Pr[<assumption_game>(<reduction>(<A>)) @ &m : res]
After inline *, both programs have the same set of random draws, differing only in order
<assumption_game> passes (g^x, g^y, g^(x*y)) to the adversary — z is NOT independently drawn
No extra masking term beyond what both sides share
No reparameterization of sampling variables
reasoning_hints: <assumption_game> uses z = <sk_sample> * <enc_rand> (deterministic), so the ciphertext group element on the <assumption_game> side is structurally equivalent to base^(<sk_sample>)^(<enc_rand>) on the <G_real> side — no additional randomness is introduced and every rnd couples via identity.
Because no extra masking or reparameterization occurs, oracle calls close with call (_: true) (identical shared state), and every rnd statement couples trivially after alignment.

The smt(expM) hint closes the group exponent equation g^a^b = g^(a*b), which arises because one side computes the product exponent directly and the other applies exponentiation twice. If smt(expM) leaves a conjunction open, use progress to decompose it and then close the algebra subgoal with by rewrite expM.

CONTRAST with the DDH1 variant of this pattern: in that variant the ciphertext group element z is independently and uniformly drawn, so a non-trivial rnd bijection is required to couple z with the masking term. Pitfalls P3 (avoid identity for reparameterized pairs) and P7 (division bijection for DDH input) apply only to DDH1-based or chosen-ciphertext proofs — they do NOT apply here.
If the planner suggests a bijection (e.g., fun z => z + <sk_sample> * <enc_rand>), that is the DDH1 recipe misapplied. For the DDH0 case, plain rnd (identity) is correct.

SWAP DIRECTION: after inline *, prefer swap{1} (the <G_real> side) over swap{2} (the <assumption_game> side). The <assumption_game> side often contains intermediate group-element assignments (computed from just-drawn values) between its rnd statements. These create read-write dependencies that cause swap{2} to fail with 'the two statements are not independent, the first statement writes X which is read by the second'. The <G_real> side's rnd statements are typically free of such intermediate assignments and can be reordered with swap{1}.
patterns.common_failure: Two failure modes: (1) Applying a non-trivial bijection rnd (e.g., fun z => z + <sk_sample> * <enc_rand>) following P3/P7 guidance, which is scoped to DDH1-based or chosen-ciphertext proofs — this creates unsolvable bijection side-conditions; use plain rnd (identity coupling) after swap alignment instead. (2) swap{2} blocked by intermediate assignments: after inline *, the <assumption_game> side often has intermediate group-element assignments (e.g., base^exponent computed from a just-sampled value) between sampling statements, creating read-write dependencies that cause swap{2} to fail with 'the two statements are not independent'. Fix: switch to swap{1} and align sampling order on the <G_real> (left) side instead.
patterns.fallback: If swap alignment is unclear after inline *, run 'python3 core/easycrypt/session_cli.py -align' to request concrete swap suggestions for the inlined goal. If swap{2} fails with a dependency error, try swap{1} to align from the <G_real> (left) side instead. If smt(expM) does not close the algebra goal alone, the goal may be a conjunction — use progress. to decompose it into individual subgoals, then close the group algebra subgoal with by rewrite expM.

patterns.structural_description: Probability inequality `Pr[<G_real> @ &m : res] <= Pr[<G_lazy> @ &m : res] + Pr[<G_lazy> @ &m : <bad_event>]` where <G_real> and <G_lazy> share the same random oracle <oracle> but differ in how they treat one specific input <bad_input>. Use a `have`-decomposition into (i) an equivalence coupling giving `res{1} => res{2} \/ <bad_event>{2}` with oracle memories agreeing everywhere except at <bad_input> (captured by `eq_except (pred1 <bad_input>) <oracle_map>{1} <oracle_map>{2}`), and (ii) a union bound on the RHS.
indicators: Goal is `Pr[<G_real> @ &m : res] <= Pr[<G_lazy> @ &m : res] + Pr[<G_lazy> @ &m : <bad_event>]`
<G_real> and <G_lazy> differ only in oracle handling of one specific input <bad_input>
File has a `declare module` adversary with losslessness axioms
Section has a random oracle <oracle> shared between games

slot_semantics.<G_real>: the real game module (starting game in the up-to-bad hop)
slot_semantics.<G_lazy>: the instrumented game that additionally tracks the bad event
slot_semantics.<oracle>: the random oracle module (its `.o` procedure is the one inlined before the `call`)
slot_semantics.<log>: the query-log set recorded by the oracle composition (e.g. Log.qs when composing Log(<oracle>))
slot_semantics.<bad_input>: the specific oracle input where the two games diverge (side-2 value)
slot_semantics.<bad_event>: the bad predicate in post-condition position (typically `<bad_input> \in <log>`)
slot_semantics.<adv_a2_ll>: losslessness lemma for the bad-event-phase adversary procedure
slot_semantics.<sampling_ll>: losslessness lemma for the oracle's sampling distribution (e.g. dptxt_ll, drand_ll)
slot_semantics.<inv_dom>: domain-subset invariant carried in the preliminary call (forall x, x \in <oracle_map> => x \in <log>)
slot_semantics.<shared_state>: tuple of shared module-state fields that remain equal on both sides through the main adversary call (e.g. seed + log)
slot_semantics.<rel_inv>: relational invariant (named op or inductive predicate) tying the two sides' oracle maps together, carried inside the main call's invariant

variants.name: two_phase_adversary_with_dom_inv
variants.conditions: (1) proof uses an intermediate `have h1: Pr[<G_real>] <= Pr[<G_lazy>: res \/ <bad_event>]` decomposition followed by a union-bound `have h2`; (2) adversary module has two procedures (pre-sampling `a1` and post-sampling `a2`), so the pRHL body has TWO `call` tactics (one 3-arg for `a2`, one 2-arg preliminary for `a1`); (3) bad event is a membership predicate `<bad_input> \in <log>`; (4) oracle coupling uses `eq_except (pred1 <bad_input>) <oracle_map>{1} <oracle_map>{2}`.

variants.name: single_phase_direct_byequiv_rel_inv
variants.conditions: (1) proof does NOT decompose via `have h1`/`have h2` — it starts directly with `byequiv (_: ... ==> res{1} => res{2} \/ <bad_event>)=> //`; (2) adversary module has a single distinguisher/main procedure (one `call` tactic in the pRHL body); (3) bad event is a named op / inductive predicate applied to shared state (e.g. `<BadOp> <log> <oracle_map>`), not a `\in` membership; (4) oracle coupling uses a custom relational invariant `<rel_inv>` (often an inductive predicate) rather than `eq_except`.

patterns.common_failure: call_before_inline_oracle: issuing `call (_: <bad_event>, Inv)` before the body of <oracle>.o is visible to EC. The 3-argument call needs to see the oracle's concrete code to check preservation / losslessness; without inlining it, EC cannot discharge the oracle subgoals.
patterns.fallback: The most common failure is calling `call (_: <bad_event>, Inv)` before inlining the oracle. EC needs to see the concrete oracle code — use selective `inline <oracle>.o` (or `inline{1} <oracle>.o; inline{2} <oracle>.o`) BEFORE the 3-arg call. Do NOT use `ler_trans` — it does not exist in EC; use the `have`-decomposition instead. If the call invariant is rejected, check that it includes BOTH the log equality (={<log>}) AND `eq_except (pred1 <bad_input>{2}) <oracle_map>{1} <oracle_map>{2}`. If a losslessness subgoal fails, search for the correct `_ll` lemma with `-search`.
reasoning_hints: Before the 3-argument call, the oracle code must be visible to EC. Use selective `inline <oracle>.o` (or `inline{1}` / `inline{2}`) to expand just the oracle. `inline *` also works but may produce many statements, making `-align` output large. If `-align` reports >20 total statements, consider undoing and using selective inline instead.

SCOPING: in `call (_: <bad_event>, Inv)`, do NOT use {1}/{2} qualifiers inside <bad_event> (first arg). Only use {1}/{2} in Inv (second arg). Representative shape: `call (_: <bad_input> \in <log>, ={<log>} /\ eq_except (pred1 <bad_input>{2}) <oracle_map>{1} <oracle_map>{2})`. Using <bad_input>{2} inside <bad_event> causes 'unknown memory: &2'.

GOAL NAVIGATION: the have-decomposition creates multiple goals; the `call` tactic must be applied on the pRHL subgoal (after `byequiv + proc`), NOT on the ambient probability goal. Use `-goal-info` to confirm you're on the pRHL goal before applying `call`.
Do NOT use `ler_trans` — it does not exist in EC. Use `have`-decomposition: `have h: Pr[<G_real>] <= Pr[<G_lazy>: res \/ <bad_event>]. + byequiv ...`

The 3-argument call form `call (_: <bad_event>, Inv)` produces 4 subgoals: (1) adversary losslessness (`exact/<adv_a2_ll>`), (2) oracle preserves Inv when <bad_event> is false (smt with eq_except lemmas), (3) oracle lossless when <bad_event> is true, (4) oracle lossless when <bad_event> is false.
The call invariant must include BOTH `={<log>}` (log equality) AND `eq_except (pred1 <bad_input>) <oracle_map>{1} <oracle_map>{2}` (memory agreement except at <bad_input>).
For subgoal 2 (oracle preserves Inv): use `smt(eq_except_set_eq eq_exceptP get_setE mem_set)` — these are the key eq_except reasoning lemmas.
For subgoals 3-4 (losslessness): use `proc; inline *; auto; smt(<sampling_ll>)` where <sampling_ll> is the losslessness lemma for the sampling distribution.
After the call, case-split on whether <bad_input> is in the oracle memory: `case: ((r \in <oracle_map>){1}).` Then use `rcondt` / `rcondf` for the conditional branch.
The union bound step (`Pr[A \/ B] <= Pr[A] + Pr[B]`) is proved by `rewrite Pr[mu_or]; smt(mu_bounded)`.

MULTI-GOAL WARNING: after `have h1: ... + byequiv => //=.`, EC may report 2-12 remaining goals. This is normal — they include side conditions from byequiv, the have continuation, and the union bound step. Do NOT restart the session; proceed with `proc. inline <oracle>.o.` on the pRHL subgoal.

PRELIMINARY CALL DOMAIN INVARIANT: when the proof has a two-phase adversary (A.a1 + A.a2) and the oracle is a logged composition, the preliminary call for A.a1 MUST include the domain-subset invariant `(forall x, x \in <oracle_map> => x \in <log>){1}`. Without it, after A.a1 completes, the main body cannot prove the else branch of the oracle conditional (where the sampled value is already in <oracle_map>): you cannot show `r \in <oracle_map> => r \in <log>` without having carried this relation as an inductive invariant. The invariant holds because the log is updated before the map. Oracle subgoal: `proc; inline *; auto=> />; smt(mem_set domE get_setE)`. See pattern `domain_invariant_logged_oracle`.

SWAP/RND BOTTOM-UP COUPLING ORDER: the `rnd` tactic closes sampling subgoals bottom-up (last remaining sampling first). After `-align`, verify that variables needed by later rnd calls do not appear after the variable handled by the earlier call; if they do, insert an extra swap to push the dependent variable past the independent one. Representative shape: for a oracle-then-encrypt scheme, the oracle-input sampler should come last so the identity coupling for the oracle reply closes first, then the message bit, then the scheme randomness.

STANDARD RESIDUAL SMT HINTS: after `inline*; auto=> />` closes program-level goals, the remaining ambient conditions are typically (1) `oget m.[x<-v].[x] = v` (`get_set_sameE`), (2) eq_except after a map update when the updated key is NOT in the predicate (`eq_except_setl`), (3) empty-map domain membership (`emptyE`). Close with `smt(get_set_sameE eq_except_setl emptyE)`.

SIM FAILURE ON ORACLE BODIES: if `sim.` fails with 'cannot infer equalities' on an oracle subgoal after inline, the oracle bodies are asymmetric — one side has extra instrumentation code. See pattern `asymmetric_oracle_coupling` for the manual decomposition (sp, peel shared structure with if/wp, handle the extra block with rcondt/rcondf + rnd or skip).

patterns.structural_description: When proving equivalence of games that use a logged lazy random oracle composition (an oracle wrapper that logs all query inputs to a set <log> before delegating to the underlying lazy random oracle <oracle>), and the proof involves a multi-phase adversary that makes oracle calls before a critical sampling step, the adversary-call invariant must include a domain-subset relation: every key in <oracle_map> was also recorded in <log>. This invariant is established by initialization (both structures start empty) and is maintained by every oracle call (the log is updated before the map). Without it, the branch where a sampled value is already in <oracle_map> cannot be discharged, because the proof cannot connect map membership to log membership.
indicators: Game uses a logged oracle composition <log_wrapper>(<oracle>) that logs all query inputs to <log>
Proof has a preliminary adversary call (e.g., A.a1) that runs before a key sampling step
After the sampling step, the proof must case-split on whether the sampled value is already in <oracle_map>
The else branch (sampled value already in <oracle_map>) requires proving the sampled value was also in <log>
Both a query log <log> and an oracle memoization map <oracle_map> appear in the game state

slot_semantics.<oracle>: the underlying lazy random oracle module
slot_semantics.<log_wrapper>: the logging oracle wrapper (e.g. Log(<oracle>)) that records queries into <log>
slot_semantics.<log>: the query-log set updated by <log_wrapper> before delegating to <oracle>
slot_semantics.<inv_dom>: the domain-subset invariant: forall x, x \in <oracle_map> => x \in <log>
slot_semantics.<rel_inv>: a named inductive predicate that bundles the domain-subset fact together with other cross-map relations (e.g. value agreement on shared keys)
slot_semantics.<bad_event>: the bad predicate used in the main 3-arg call when the invariant rides along with a bad-event coupling

variants.name: preliminary_call_forall_inv
variants.conditions: (1) adversary has two phases (pre-sampling and post-sampling); (2) proof emits a SEPARATE 2-arg `call` tactic for the preliminary phase, distinct from the main 3-arg bad-event call; (3) domain-subset invariant is stated as an explicit `forall x, x \in <oracle_map> => x \in <log>` conjunct in that preliminary call.

variants.name: inductive_rel_inv_inside_main_call
variants.conditions: (1) adversary is single-phase (no separate preliminary call); (2) game uses a logged oracle wrapper that appends queries onto <log> before writing <oracle_map>; (3) the proof carries the domain-subset fact packaged inside a named inductive predicate <rel_inv> that also encodes value-agreement on shared keys; (4) <rel_inv> sits inside the main 3-arg bad-event `call (_: <bad_event>, Inv)`; NO separate preliminary call exists.

patterns.common_failure: domain_invariant_missing_from_preliminary_call: the preliminary call is issued with just `={glob <log_wrapper>, glob <oracle>}` (without the domain-subset clause). The call itself succeeds; the failure appears later at the else branch of the <oracle> conditional, where the goal `x \in <oracle_map>{1} => x \in <log>{1}` cannot be proved. Fix: restart the byequiv proof and include <inv_dom> in the preliminary call invariant.
patterns.fallback: If the else branch of the <oracle> conditional (where the queried value is already in <oracle_map>) is not provable — specifically, cannot show a value in <oracle_map> is also in <log> — the preliminary adversary call is missing the domain-subset invariant. Restart the byequiv proof and include `(forall x, x \in <oracle_map> => x \in <log>){1}` in the preliminary call invariant.
reasoning_hints: WHY THE INVARIANT HOLDS: after init(), both <oracle_map> and <log> start empty. Every call to <log_wrapper>.o inserts the query input into <log> first, then into <oracle_map>. So `dom(<oracle_map>) ⊆ <log>` is an inductive invariant of any execution that starts from init().
WHEN NEEDED: include this invariant whenever you call an adversary phase that precedes a sampling step where the proof must reason about <oracle_map> containing (or not containing) the sampled value.

CONNECTION TO UP-TO-BAD: this pattern typically appears as the preliminary call (e.g., A.a1) inside a larger `up_to_bad_eq_except` proof. The main bad-event call (A.a2) uses a 3-argument bad-event coupling; the preliminary call uses a 2-argument coupling that carries the domain relation forward.

ORACLE SUBGOAL HINTS: the oracle-preserves-invariant subgoal is closed by `proc; inline *; auto=> />; smt(mem_set domE get_setE)`. Key lemmas: `mem_set` (x ∈ m.[k <- v] ↔ x = k ∨ x ∈ m), `domE` (x ∈ dom m ↔ m.[x] ≠ None), `get_setE` (m.[k <- v].[x] = if x = k then v else m.[x]).

PITFALL: omitting this invariant is NOT caught at the call site — the call succeeds with just `={glob <log_wrapper>, glob <oracle>}`. The failure appears later at the else branch of the <oracle> conditional. The fix requires restarting the byequiv proof and adding the invariant.

patterns.structural_description: When auto or auto=> /> leaves multiple ambient subgoals involving maps, eq_except, or other first-order logic with if-then-else branches, decompose with progress. before attempting smt. Large ambient goals with conditional branches and map operations rarely close with a single smt call — progress. splits them into individually provable pieces that each need only one targeted smt hint.
indicators: auto or auto=> /> accepted but goals remain (goal count > 0)
Remaining goal has if-then-else, map operations (oget, .[_], .[_ <- _]), or eq_except
A single smt call with many hints fails, times out, or returns STRICT_WARNING
Goal body is large (>10 lines) with nested forall/implies/conjunction
typical_tactics: Step 2 — Close each subgoal individually with targeted smt:
smt(get_set_sameE).  (* map lookup after set: oget m.[x <- v].[x] = v *)
smt(eq_except_setl).  (* eq_except after map update at the excepted key *)
smt(eq_except_refl).  (* eq_except reflexivity: eq_except p m m *)
smt().  (* plain smt for simple logic or arithmetic *)
patterns.common_failure: single_large_smt
patterns.fallback: If progress. generates too many subgoals (>10), apply auto=> /> first to simplify, then progress. on what remains. If individual smt calls fail, try adding domain lemmas: domE, mem_set, get_setE, fdom_set. If a subgoal involves oget on a key you just set, get_set_sameE is almost always the right hint.
reasoning_hints: progress. is the ambient-logic analogue of proc;inline*;auto for pRHL — it breaks a compound conjunction/implication into manageable pieces

Common smt hints for map goals: get_set_sameE (m.[x<-v].[x] = v), get_setE (case split on key equality), eq_except_setl (updating at the excepted key preserves eq_except), domE (domain membership), mem_set (set membership after set insertion)

If you have been idle for >30s trying to find the right smt hints for an ambient goal, stop and use progress. first — decomposing is almost always faster than guessing the right hint combination for a large goal

patterns.structural_description: Prove Pr[E \/ F] <= Pr[E] + Pr[F] in an ambient probability goal. This commonly arises after a have-decomposition where h1 bounds by Pr[A \/ B] but the main goal needs the bound in terms of Pr[A] + Pr[B]. Direct smt on the combined goal often fails because the solver must both rewrite the disjunction probability and perform arithmetic — splitting into two steps is more reliable.
indicators: Goal involves Pr[...] <= Pr[...] + Pr[...]
A hypothesis h1 bounds by Pr[A \/ B] but the goal needs Pr[A] + Pr[B]
Direct smt(mu_or mu_bounded) fails or gives STRICT_WARNING
Goal has the form: h1: X <= Pr[G: A \/ B] |- X <= Pr[G: A] + Pr[G: B]
typical_tactics: Step 1 — Introduce the union bound as a separate hypothesis:
have h2: Pr[G.main() @ &m : A \/ B] <= Pr[G.main() @ &m : A] + Pr[G.main() @ &m : B].
Step 2 — Prove h2 using the probability union bound lemma:
Step 3 — Close main goal by transitivity (h1 and h2 are both in context):
patterns.common_failure: direct_smt_union_bound
patterns.fallback: If rewrite Pr[mu_or] fails, the event expression may not syntactically match mu_or's pattern. Try: smt(Pr_mu_or mu_bounded) or apply mu_or manually. If smt() cannot combine h1 and h2 at Step 3, verify both hypotheses are in the current context (not discharged by a prior subgoal-closing tactic).
reasoning_hints: mu_or is: Pr[A \/ B] = Pr[A] + Pr[B] - Pr[A /\ B]. Combined with mu_bounded (Pr[E] >= 0 for any E), this yields Pr[A \/ B] <= Pr[A] + Pr[B].

The have-decomposition approach works because smt can handle simple real arithmetic when the hypotheses are in context, but struggles to combine rewriting (Pr[mu_or]) with arithmetic (mu_bounded) and transitivity (h1) in a single step.
After Step 2, the remaining goal has form: h1: X <= Pr[A \/ B], h2: Pr[A \/ B] <= Pr[A] + Pr[B] |- X <= Pr[A] + Pr[B]. This is just real-valued transitivity — plain smt() handles it.

patterns.structural_description: Prove `Pr[<G_real> @ &m : res] <= Pr[<G_lazy> @ &m : res] + Pr[<G_lazy> @ &m : <bad_event>]` between two games that differ only when a bad event holds, via a probabilistic coupling with a relational invariant. The core move is the 3-arg bad-event `call (_: <bad_event>, <rel_inv>)`, which generates 1 + 4K subgoals for K oracles (adversary losslessness, per-oracle relational equiv when <bad_event> is false, per-oracle phoare <bad_event>-preservation, and per-oracle losslessness). Two variants differ in how the outer Pr-inequality is attacked: (A) `direct_byequiv_inductive_bad` — when <bad_event> is a user-defined inductive predicate with named constructors and <rel_inv> is a custom relational predicate, `byequiv` is applied directly on the inequality with coupling post `res{1} => res{2} \/ (<bad_event>){2}`; (B) `have_decomposition_with_eq_except` — when <bad_event> is a membership `x \in S` and <rel_inv> is a map-level `eq_except`, prefix with `have h1: Pr[<G_real>] <= Pr[<G_lazy>: res \/ <bad_event>]` and close with a `Pr[mu_or]` union bound.
indicators: Goal is `Pr[<G_real> @ &m : res] <= Pr[<G_lazy> @ &m : res] + Pr[<G_lazy> @ &m : <bad_event>]`
Games share the same oracles but <G_lazy> additionally exposes the bad event
A relational invariant <rel_inv> connects the two game states when <bad_event> has not occurred
<bad_event> captures an exceptional condition (cycle / collision / membership) that voids the coupling

slot_semantics.<G_real>: the starting game module (the template expands as `<G_real>.main()`)
slot_semantics.<G_lazy>: the instrumented game module that additionally tracks <bad_event>
slot_semantics.<bad_event>: a global-state predicate for the bad event (inductive predicate like `Bad <log> <map>` in variant A, or a membership like `<x> \in <S>` in variant B) — must not carry a {1}/{2} qualifier when used as the first arg of `call`
slot_semantics.<rel_inv>: the relational invariant relating the two game states when <bad_event> is false (may use {1}/{2})
slot_semantics.<oracle_ll>: per-oracle losslessness lemma (one per oracle in the game)
slot_semantics.<dist_ll_1>: losslessness lemma for the first sampling distribution inside an oracle (e.g. dseed_ll, dptxt_ll)
slot_semantics.<dist_ll_2>: losslessness lemma for the second sampling distribution inside an oracle (e.g. dout_ll)
slot_semantics.<Ctor1>: first constructor of the inductive <bad_event> type (variant A only)
slot_semantics.<Ctor2>: second constructor of the inductive <bad_event> type (variant A only)
slot_semantics.<oracle>: the oracle module name whose `o` method is inlined on the <G_lazy> side (variant B only; e.g. LRO)

variants.name: direct_byequiv_inductive_bad
variants.conditions: The goal is attacked by `byequiv` directly — no outer `have h1` decomposition, no `Pr[mu_or]` union bound. Prerequisites: (i) <bad_event> is declared in the file as an inductive type with >=2 named constructors; (ii) <rel_inv> is a user-defined relational predicate destructured via `move: H_inv => [...]` inside subgoals; (iii) adversary + each oracle have known losslessness lemmas. Characteristic opener: `byequiv ... proc. call (_: <bad_event>, <rel_inv>).`

variants.name: have_decomposition_with_eq_except
variants.conditions: The goal is proved in two stages: first `have h1: Pr[<G_real>] <= Pr[<G_lazy>: res \/ <bad_event>]` via byequiv, then `have h2: Pr[<G_lazy>: res \/ <bad_event>] <= Pr[<G_lazy>: res] + Pr[<G_lazy>: <bad_event>]` via `rewrite Pr[mu_or]`. Prerequisites: (i) <bad_event> is a simple membership / boolean expression (no inductive constructors); (ii) <rel_inv> is a map-level `eq_except` invariant between the two oracle maps; (iii) <G_lazy> side exposes an oracle module <oracle> whose `o` method is inlined immediately after `proc`.

patterns.common_failure: bad_pred_uses_side_qualifier: `call (_: <bad_event>, <rel_inv>)` is rejected with 'unknown memory' when <bad_event> is written with a {2} qualifier. The first argument to the 3-arg call must be a global-state predicate (no {1}/{2}); only <rel_inv> (second arg) may use {1}/{2}. Secondary failure: closing `weight d = 1%r` with `smt(<dist_ll>)` — smt may silently stall where `exact <dist_ll>` succeeds.
patterns.fallback: If `call (_: <bad_event>, <rel_inv>)` is rejected with 'unknown memory', remove any {2} qualifier from <bad_event>. If phoare bad-preservation goals cannot be closed by `auto` alone, manually introduce the quantifiers with `move=>` and decompose the weight/forall chain step by step. For constructor case-splits on <bad_event>, use `case: h_bad => [...]` to name constructor arguments before applying smt.
reasoning_hints: VARIANT SELECTION: does the file declare <bad_event> as an inductive type with named constructors and <rel_inv> as a custom relational predicate? Use variant `direct_byequiv_inductive_bad`. Is <bad_event> a `x \in S` membership and <rel_inv> an `eq_except (pred1 <x>) m1 m2`? Use `have_decomposition_with_eq_except`.

BAD PREDICATE SCOPING: the first argument to `call (_: <bad_event>, <rel_inv>)` must NOT use a {2} memory qualifier. It represents a predicate over the global state before the call, not a side-specific memory. Writing `<bad_event>{2}` in the first arg causes 'unknown memory: &2'. <rel_inv> (second arg) freely uses {1}/{2}. In the byequiv post-condition, wrap as `(<bad_event>){2}` so the {2} applies to the whole expression.

PHOARE BAD-PRESERVATION SUBGOAL SHAPE: after `proc; auto.` on a phoare bad-preservation goal, the residual has the shape `weight d1 = 1%r /\ forall v, v \in d1 => predT v <=> weight d2 = 1%r /\ forall v0, v0 \in d2 => predT v0 <=> <bad_event> new_state`. Discharge manually: `move=> &2 [[h_bad _] _]; split; first exact <dist_ll_1>. move=> v _; rewrite /predT /=; split; first exact <dist_ll_2>. move=> v0 _; case: branch_cond => h; case: h_bad => [? | r ??]; [apply <Ctor1>; smt | apply (<Ctor2> r); smt(mem_set)].` CRITICAL: use `exact <dist_ll>` (not `smt(<dist_ll>)`) for `weight d = 1%r` sub-goals — smt may silently fail here.

INVARIANT DESTRUCTURING: when <rel_inv> is an inductive predicate, destruct it before using in smt: `move: H_inv => [h_field1 h_field2]`. Without destructuring, smt cannot reason about user-defined predicates. See `inductive_type_smt_barrier` for the general pattern.
SUBGOAL COUNT: for a game with K oracles, expect 1 + 4K subgoals from `call (_: <bad_event>, <rel_inv>)`. For 2 oracles: 9 subgoals. Track them by goal count using `-goal-info` between transitions.

COMPARISON TO up_to_bad_eq_except: that pattern is the specialized RO-with-eq_except form (same skeleton as this pattern's variant B). This pattern is the generalization; its variant A extends the recipe to any user-defined inductive bad event + custom relational invariant.

SIM FAILURE ON ORACLE BODIES: if `sim.` fails on an oracle subgoal because <G_lazy> has extra conditional/sampling blocks, see pattern `asymmetric_oracle_coupling`. Do not retry `sim.`; manually decompose past the shared structure to the divergence point.

patterns.structural_description: When an EasyCrypt proof goal involves a user-defined inductive predicate or type — either as a hypothesis to be used or as a goal to be constructed — automated SMT solving consistently fails because the SMT backend treats user-defined inductive types as opaque. The fix is always to destruct inductive hypotheses into their component parts before invoking automated solvers, and to apply constructors explicitly when building inductive goals. This applies equally to record-like predicates (defined as a conjunction of conditions) and sum-type bad events (with multiple constructors).
indicators: smt() or smt(hints) silently fails (no-progress, no error) on a goal that looks provable
A hypothesis in context has a user-defined inductive predicate type (e.g., `<rel_inv> a b c`, `<bad_event> state mem`)
The goal or context contains constructor calls from a user-defined inductive type
progress. reveals sub-goals involving user-defined predicates that smt cannot discharge

slot_semantics.<bad_event>: a user-defined inductive bad-event predicate (typically a sum type with named constructors)
slot_semantics.<Ctor1>: first constructor of the inductive type
slot_semantics.<Ctor2>: second constructor of the inductive type
slot_semantics.<rel_inv>: a user-defined inductive relational invariant predicate in context

patterns.common_failure: smt_on_opaque_inductive: calling `smt()` or `smt(hints)` on a goal that still carries a user-defined inductive hypothesis. The SMT backend treats the inductive opaquely; it produces no progress and no error message. The fix is to destructure the hypothesis first.
patterns.fallback: If destructuring reveals nested inductives, apply the approach recursively: destruct each layer before calling smt. If a predicate is abstract (declared but not defined in scope), search for destructor or projection lemmas in the file using `-search`.
reasoning_hints: ROOT CAUSE: EasyCrypt's smt backend (Why3 + Z3 / Alt-Ergo) treats user-defined inductive types as opaque. It cannot unfold definitions or apply constructors automatically. After destructuring, sub-goals involve only standard types (bool, int, fmap, fset) that the solver handles natively.

DETECTION SIGNAL: `smt()` gives no-progress with no error message on a goal that appears provable. Check whether any hypothesis or goal term is a user-defined inductive predicate — this is the most reliable indicator.

RECORD-LIKE PREDICATES: a predicate defined as `pred <rel_inv> s1 s2 log = (cond1 s1 s2) /\ (cond2 s2 log)` is destructured with `move: H => [h1 h2]`. Each component is a standard formula smt can reason about.

SUM-TYPE BAD EVENTS: an inductive `type <bad_event> = <Ctor1> of ... | <Ctor2> of ...` requires `case: H_bad => [args1 | args2]` to split into constructor cases. Handle each constructor separately before calling smt.

CONSTRUCTION: to prove `<rel_inv> new_s1 new_s2 new_log`, do NOT use `smt(<Ctor>)` — the SMT backend does not recognize constructors as proof hints. Instead: `split; [prove_field1 | prove_field2]` or `apply <Ctor>` followed by sub-goal discharge.
RELATED: the `up_to_bad_fundamental_lemma` pattern frequently triggers this barrier in oracle-equiv and phoare bad-preservation subgoals.

patterns.structural_description: In a pRHL proof, after inlining oracle bodies, sim. fails because one side has an extra conditional block with sampling that the other side lacks. This is common in game-hopping proofs where an instrumented game (G2) adds tracking code — bad-event flags, counters, extra sampling for detection — inside oracle bodies that are otherwise identical to the original game (G1). The extra block does not change the observable output but adds state updates, making the programs syntactically asymmetric. sim. requires structural symmetry and cannot handle this divergence.
indicators: sim. fails with 'cannot infer the set of equalities' on a pRHL oracle body subgoal
After inline *, one side has an extra if-block or procedure call that the other lacks
The extra block samples a value and/or updates a flag/counter (bad-event tracking)
Both oracle bodies share the same initial structure (assignments, RO calls) but diverge at the instrumentation point
-goal-info reports oracle mismatch: left calls X.O.proc, right calls Y.O.proc
typical_tactics: Step 1 — Do NOT use sim. Use sp to consume shared initial assignments, then peel shared structure with if/wp/call.
Step 2 — At the divergence point (extra block on one side): use seq to split before/after, or rcondt/rcondf to enter/skip the conditional on the instrumented side.
Step 3 — Inside the extra block: rnd to couple extra sampling (if the sampled value appears in the postcondition), or wp;skip if the extra state is not in the postcondition.
Step 4 — After the extra block, the programs reconverge. Close the remaining shared tail with wp; auto or sim (if the tail is now syntactically equal).
Step 5 — The postcondition typically requires showing a monotone implication (flag_before => flag_after). Use case analysis on the flag state.
patterns.common_failure: sim_on_asymmetric_oracle
patterns.fallback: If you cannot identify the divergence point visually, use -align to see where the statement counts differ between left and right. The first position where the two sides have different statement types marks the divergence. If the asymmetric block involves a nested procedure call (<@), inline it first to expose the raw code.
reasoning_hints: DIAGNOSIS: When sim. fails after inline *, compare the two programs visually or via -align. Look for a conditional or procedure call on ONE side that has no counterpart on the other. This is the instrumentation point.

WHY sim. FAILS: sim. auto-infers a coupling (set of equalities) by structurally matching left and right statements. When one side has extra statements (conditional, sampling, assignment), there is nothing to match them against, and sim. reports 'cannot infer the set of equalities'.

COMMON SHAPES: (a) Extra `if (counter = index) { v <$ dist; flag <- flag || v = target; }` — the game checks whether the current operation matches a tracked index and sets a flag. (b) Extra `log <- log ++ [entry]` — the game records an event for later analysis. (c) Extra `t <@ set_bad(args)` — a sub-procedure that samples and conditionally sets a bad-event flag.

HANDLING THE EXTRA BLOCK: If the extra block has a conditional `if (cond) { ... }`, use `rcondt` or `rcondf` on that side to enter or skip it, depending on what the invariant implies about `cond`. If the extra block samples a value used in the invariant, couple it with `rnd`. If the sampled value is purely local (not in invariant or postcondition), `wp; skip` suffices.

MONOTONE INVARIANT: In bad-event tracking, the invariant typically includes an implication like `(event_left => flag_right)`. This is monotone: once the flag is set, it stays set. When the extra block sets the flag, the implication is preserved vacuously. When it doesn't, the implication holds by the induction hypothesis.

ALTERNATIVE: Instead of inline * on the whole oracle, try call (_: Inv) at the oracle level with an explicit invariant that abstracts the asymmetric part. This avoids expanding the oracle body entirely, but requires knowing the right invariant upfront.

closer_idioms.trigger: pRHL equiv subgoal just past `byequiv=> //; proc` where the body contains an adversary/oracle `call`; the two sides share a local invariant on state variables and the remaining code is syntactically identical after the call.

slot_semantics.<invariant>: the pRHL relational invariant relating the two sides' visible state (typically `={glob X, Y}` or `={state1, state2}`).

closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: After `proc` opens a pRHL equiv, the wrapper around the adversary/oracle `call` needs a relational assertion; after that the tails are lockstep and `sim` discharges them. `by sim` also handles any residual `skip` obligation.

closer_idioms.trigger: Oracle-equivalence subgoal produced by `call (_: ...)` where the oracle body has no relevant side effects on the relational part (often a simple lookup/update shared by both sides).
closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: When both oracles share the same implementation (or differ only in code that `auto` can reconcile), `proc; inline *; auto` walks the body and discharges the equiv without a custom invariant.

closer_idioms.trigger: Ambient logic (non-pRHL) residual goal on pure arithmetic/boolean reasoning; remaining obligations are within `smt`'s reach after `auto`'s split.
closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: `auto` discharges sampling/wp; `=> />` simplifies introduced hypotheses; `/#` is the `smt` shortcut that closes the remaining arithmetic/logic obligation. Works when no crypto-specific lemma is needed.

closer_idioms.trigger: Terminal subgoal after `auto` still requires knowing that a specific distribution is lossless (for `byphoare` / while-loop termination / trivial oracle ll).

slot_semantics.<sampling_ll>: name of the lossless lemma for the distribution being sampled (e.g. `dt_ll`, `dbool_ll`, `dptxt_ll`, `drand_ll`).

closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: `auto` discharges sampling/wp; `smt(<sampling_ll>)` then closes the mass/positivity residue by feeding the losslessness lemma as a hint.

closer_idioms.trigger: pRHL equiv where two samplings must be related by an invertible additive mask; typical pattern: one side samples `z` uniformly, the other samples `z + k` uniformly, and the group is uniform.

slot_semantics.<mask_expr>: the (side-tagged) masking term added on the left, e.g. `x0{1} * (if b{1} then m1{1} else m0{1})` or `e{1} * w0{1}`.

closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: The `rnd (f) (g)` couple form supplies both directions of the bijection explicitly; EC then generates side-conditions requiring `f;g = id` and `g;f = id`, which are closed by `algebra`/`smt` on the additive-group axioms.

closer_idioms.trigger: pRHL equiv where one side samples `h` and the other samples `h +^ m`; need to couple via XOR with a masking message.

slot_semantics.<mask_expr>: the (side-tagged) masking expression XOR-ed in, e.g. `if b then m1 else m0` or a choice-of-message term.

closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: Because `+^` is an involution (`x +^ m +^ m = x`), the single-function `rnd f` form is enough — EC derives the inverse direction automatically. The residual is `algebra` / `smt(addpK ...)` style.

closer_idioms.trigger: Terminal `Pr[<G_mid> @ &m : res] = 1%r/2%r` obligation after `byphoare=> //; proc` where `res` is `b' = b` and `b` is the last uniformly-sampled bit independent of the adversary's guess.

slot_semantics.<challenge_bit>: the final uniformly-sampled challenge-bit variable in the game (typically `b` or `b'`).

closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: `rnd (pred1 <challenge_bit>)` reduces the probability to `mu_ pred1 = dbool1E = 1/2`. Follow up with `auto` / `smt(dt_ll DBool.dbool1E)` to close the mass and independence obligations.

closer_idioms.trigger: Concluding equality `|Pr[<G_real>] - 1/2| = |Pr[<assumption_game>0] - Pr[<assumption_game>1]|` (or similar) that needs to be closed by rewriting the three bridge lemmas proved earlier in the section.
closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: Final-assembly step: each bridge lemma is instantiated at `&m` and used as a rewrite (possibly backward with `-`). Order and direction must match so the two sides collapse to the same expression.

closer_idioms.trigger: Terminal equality `Pr[<G_left> @ &m : P] = Pr[<G_right> @ &m : P]` (or an inequality of matching shape) whose inner equiv has already been proved as a local `equiv` lemma.

slot_semantics.<oracle_equiv_lemma>: name of the pre-existing equiv lemma bridging the two games (e.g. `eq_Game1_Game2`, `CPA_G0`, `D6_Sample`).

closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: `byequiv` converts a `Pr[_] = Pr[_]` obligation into the equiv stored in the bridge lemma; `by` discharges the standard pre/post compatibility side-conditions, typically `true ==> true` or auto-unifiable.

closer_idioms.trigger: Terminal `Pr[_] = Pr[_]` obligation where the main equiv is closed by `sim` and an auxiliary smt side-goal remains (typically post-mortem state compatibility).

slot_semantics.<post>: the equiv post-condition the bridge should preserve (e.g. `={arg, glob X} ==> ={<program>.s}` or `={glob X, glob Y}`).

closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: The parenthesized form `byequiv (: ...)` injects a tailored post/pre so the resulting equiv is closed by `sim`; `first by sim` handles the main equiv branch while the secondary `Pr` side condition falls through to `smt()`/`/#` downstream.

closer_idioms.trigger: After an additive-bijection `rnd (fun d => d + <mask_expr>) (fun d => d - <mask_expr>)` coupling, three obligations remain: cancellation (`f;g = id`), mass-preservation of the uniform distribution, and image preservation.

slot_semantics.<sampling_ll>: the uniform/lossless lemma name for the domain distribution (e.g. `dt_ll`, `dseed_ll`).
slot_semantics.<group_lemmas>: name list for the underlying group-axiom lemmas (e.g. `expD expM`, `ZModpField.addrK ZModpField.subrK`).

closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: After `rnd (f) (g)` produces three side-conditions, `skip` closes the WP, `=> />` normalizes, `do!split` peels the conjunction, and `smt` discharges each piece using the uniform-lossless lemma plus the appropriate group axioms.

closer_idioms.trigger: Phoare subgoal `Pr[G.main() @ &m : res] = 1/2` reduced by `byphoare=> //; proc; rnd (pred1 <b>)` where the body calls a two-stage adversary (`choose`/`guess`) whose losslessness is declared as axioms.

slot_semantics.<guess_ll>: name of the axiom/lemma witnessing `islossless A.guess` (or the second adversary stage).
slot_semantics.<choose_ll>: name of the axiom/lemma witnessing `islossless A.choose` (or the first adversary stage).

closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: After `rnd (pred1 b')`, the remaining program is `rnd; call A.choose; rnd; call A.guess; rnd` (reverse-of-program-order since we walk backwards under wp). Each `call X_ll` discharges a lossless adversary call; `rnd` / `skip` close the sampling side-conditions.

closer_idioms.trigger: Terminal goal `Pr[G.main() @ &m : res] = 1%r/2%r` where `G` is a simple guessing game that samples one final bit after calling the adversary.

slot_semantics.<challenge_bit>: the final uniformly-sampled challenge-bit variable whose value `res` collapses onto (typically `b'`).

closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: Compressed one-shot close for dice/coin-guessing probability equal to 1/2: `byphoare` converts `Pr = 1/2` into a phoare judgment, `proc` opens the body, `rnd (pred1 b')` reduces the final sampling to `mu_ pred1`, and `auto=> />` closes the mass obligation.

closer_idioms.trigger: Oracle subgoal opened by `call (_: <op> <args>)` where the invariant is a named operator/predicate. `smt()` alone cannot close because `<op>` stays opaque; unfolding it exposes the conjuncts that smt/auto can then dispatch. Typically the oracle body is `sp`-alignable prefix + `if` then branch-discharge.

slot_semantics.<op>: the named operator used as the call's invariant (must match an `op <op> ... = <conjunction>` definition in the file)
slot_semantics.<L>: number of statements to consume on side 1 before the `if` (read from the current pRHL goal)
slot_semantics.<R>: number of statements to consume on side 2 before the `if`

closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: After `call (_: <op> args)`, the post-condition contains `<op> args` opaquely. `rewrite /<op>` unfolds it to its definition (a conjunction of relational facts). `proc => /=` enters the oracle's proc body with beta-reduction. `sp L R` consumes aligned assignment prefixes. `if` splits the if-condition into 3 subgoals; branch 1 is typically trivially from the invariant (`done`), branch 2 discharged by `auto`.

closer_idioms.trigger: Inside a pRHL sub-proof, after inline/sp have exposed an oracle call-site on one side, when an `oracle_equiv` lemma (`equiv <NAME>`) discharges it via `ecall`. The resulting ambient residual typically needs a single predicate-unfold + `/#` close.

slot_semantics.<oracle_equiv_lemma>: a local `equiv <NAME>` lemma whose conclusion matches the current call-site's procedure pair
slot_semantics.<call_site_vars>: the concrete arguments passed to ecall; read them from the current pRHL goal call-site
slot_semantics.<predicate>: a named predicate (`op <predicate> = ...`) that gates the oracle's behavior; unfolding it exposes the boolean conditions the ambient residual needs

closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: `ecall` instantiates the equiv lemma on the current call-site's arguments, leaving an ambient residual that captures the lemma's preconditions. Unfolding `<predicate>` exposes the boolean conjuncts the preconditions depend on; `auto => />` simplifies; `/#` closes by linear arithmetic + boolean reasoning.

closer_idioms.trigger: phoare goal `[<proc> : <pre> ==> <post>] <= <bound>` where the program admits sequential decomposition: prove the first K statements maintain an intermediate predicate with one bound, the remaining statements with another. Typical entry: after `byphoare => //. proc.` peeled the program to a sequence; the standalone `seq K : (INV).` (pRHL form) is rejected because the goal is now phoare and needs explicit bound factors.

slot_semantics.<K>: split position (number of statements consumed by the prefix)
slot_semantics.<intermediate_predicate>: intermediate predicate that holds after the first K statements
slot_semantics.<bound1>..<bound4>: the four bound factors that PSeqMult/PSeqAdd rules consume — exact semantics depends on which rule EC picks; canonical 4-arg shape documented in the relevant EasyCrypt reference...) 1%r 1%r 0%r _ => //=`)

closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: Phoare seq differs from pRHL seq: the phoare form takes BOUND FACTORS in addition to the intermediate invariant. The 4-bound shape (the relevant EasyCrypt reference canonical) is the most general PSeqMult form; simpler PSeqAdd variants exist with fewer bounds. Do NOT use the pRHL `seq K L : (INV)` form on a phoare goal — it'll error on the missing bound argument. eca) before discovering the 4-bound form because no KB or tactic-forms entry surfaced it.

closer_idioms.trigger: phoare bound proof on a procedure containing a `while` loop, where the bound takes the form `qN%r * pr_event` (max iterations × per-iteration probability of a bad event). The goal shape is `[<proc> : <pre> ==> <bad_event>] <= <max_iters>%r * <per_iter_bound>` after `byphoare`.

slot_semantics.<pos>: statement position to instrument (typically the position of the loop or counter assignment)
slot_semantics.<counter>: counter variable that increments each loop iteration (must be present in scope)
slot_semantics.<step_fn>: function from current counter value to bound for that iteration: `fun x => <expr involving x>`
slot_semantics.<max_iters>: upper bound on the counter (the qN parameter)
slot_semantics.<bad_event>: predicate over global state that 'bad' fires

closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: The failure-event lemma family bounds cumulative bad-event probability across a bounded loop by summing per-iteration contributions. It generates obligations for bad-event preservation and for the per-iteration new-failure bound. Use the tactic-forms helper to confirm the exact arity expected by the local EasyCrypt version.

closer_idioms.trigger: Goal of shape `Pr[<game>(<args>).<proc>() @ &m : <E1> \/ <E2>] <= <bound>` (or the symmetric `<bound> >= Pr[...]`). Disjunction at the top level of the Pr event, single Pr term in the goal, bound side does not contain another Pr.

slot_semantics.<game>: the program whose execution is being bounded
slot_semantics.<E1>: first disjunct of the event predicate (boolean expression over post-state)
slot_semantics.<E2>: second disjunct (further `\/` chains generalize the same way)
slot_semantics.<bound>: arithmetic upper-bound expression — typically `q * pr_event` or similar
slot_semantics.<pre>: phoare precondition; `true` works when the bound holds unconditionally

closer_idioms.reasoning_hints: EC's `mu_or` rewrites `mu d (predU p q) = mu d p + mu d q - mu d (predI p q)` — but `Pr[A : res]` decomposes to `mu (mu_x A) (fun r => res r)` and the body has no `predU` outermost. `rewrite Pr [mu_or]` therefore reports `Pr-rewrite: cannot find a pattern for mu_or`. The canonical path is `byphoare`, which carries the disjunction into a phoare postcondition where it can be handled by `phoare split <bound1> <bound2>` (independent bounds), `conseq` refactoring, or a direct proof if one disjunct is trivially false at the relevant program point.
